title: Balanced Scorecard
definition: The Balanced Scorecard (BSC) is a strategic management and performance-measurement framework developed by Robert Kaplan (Harvard) and David Norton in their 1992 _Harvard Business Review_ article and 1996 book _The Balanced Scorecard: Translating Strategy into Action_. It corrects the historical over-reliance on lagging financial indicators by linking financial measures with three additional perspectives — Customer, Internal Business Processes, and Learning & Growth — that act as leading indicators of future financial performance. Pereira frames it through a pilot analogy: managing a business on financial dashboards alone is like flying a plane with one instrument; the BSC gives executives a multi-dimensional cockpit view.

sections.Definition: The Balanced Scorecard (BSC) is a strategic management and performance-measurement framework developed by Robert Kaplan (Harvard) and David Norton in their 1992 _Harvard Business Review_ article and 1996 book _The Balanced Scorecard: Translating Strategy into Action_. It corrects the historical over-reliance on lagging financial indicators by linking financial measures with three additional perspectives — Customer, Internal Business Processes, and Learning & Growth — that act as leading indicators of future financial performance. Pereira frames it through a pilot analogy: managing a business on financial dashboards alone is like flying a plane with one instrument; the BSC gives executives a multi-dimensional cockpit view.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Four-perspective architecture** — Financial, Customer, Internal Processes, Learning & Growth, each with its own objectives and KPIs (page 21)
- **Leading + lagging indicators** — Learning & Growth, Internal Processes, and Customer are leading; Financial is lagging (page 35)
- **Cause-and-effect logic** — perspectives build on each other from the bottom (Learning & Growth) up to Financial; not four parallel silos (page 34)
- **Strategy translation, not just measurement** — converts vision/mission into testable objectives and concrete KPIs (page 16)
- **Top-leader sponsorship required** — demands CEO or top-leader participation; cannot be delegated to a working group (page 22, 60)
- **Few metrics by design** — forces focus on the handful of indicators that genuinely move strategy (page 12, 32)
- **Adaptable across sectors** — originally private-sector; now used by public sector, nonprofits, government agencies, sports teams (page 14, 18)
- **Total management overhaul, not a tool drop-in** — Pereira: cannot be "finished in a few weeks or months"; requires committing to change how you manage (page 13)
sections.When to use:
- Translating a strategic vision into measurable execution across a multi-unit organization
- When financial reports alone are not telling the full performance story (e.g. revenue is fine but customer churn or technical debt is rising)
- During major strategic shifts (new market, M&A integration, turnaround) where alignment across units matters
- Public-sector or nonprofit contexts where mission outcomes need to coexist with financial sustainability
- When the board or investors are asking for a coherent performance-reporting story
sections.When NOT to use:
- In small startups where the strategy is still being discovered (use OKRs or lean experiments instead)
- When leadership will not personally sponsor and engage (Pereira: BSC fails without leadership buy-in, page 60)
- When the organization lacks the data infrastructure to feed KPIs reliably (the BSC "requires a lot of data," page 61)
- When the goal is short-term tactical course-correction rather than multi-year strategy execution
- If you cannot resist the urge to track everything — BSC value collapses under metric sprawl
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Treating the four perspectives as equal/parallel** instead of as a cause-and-effect stack (Pereira's "fundamental" mistake, page 34)
- **Copying another company's scorecard** — Kaplan & Norton are explicit: must be tailored to your market, products, competitive pressure (page 16, 60)
- **Too many KPIs** — FranklinCovey citation: 3 goals → all achieved; 4–10 → one or two; 10+ → none (page 33)
- **All-lagging measures** because they are easiest to count, leaving nothing actionable to change (page 33)
- **Treating BSC as a measurement system** rather than a strategic management system (Kaplan & Norton's repeated warning, page 16)
- **No leadership air cover** — initiative dies in the middle layer (page 60)
- **Data fatigue** — managers resent filling out scorecards because it crowds out "real work" (page 61–62)
sections.Key metrics:
- **Strategy execution rate** — % of strategic objectives on track quarter-over-quarter
- **Perspective balance** — distribution of objectives across the four perspectives (no perspective neglected)
- **Leading vs lagging ratio** — share of KPIs that are forward-looking (Pereira: most plans over-index on lagging, page 33)
- **KPI alignment score** — % of unit/team KPIs that ladder up to a corporate strategic objective
- **Cascading depth** — number of org levels with their own scorecard tied to the corporate scorecard
sections.Decision questions:
- Do we have a real strategy to execute, or are we using the BSC to invent one? (BSC translates strategy; it does not create it)
- Who is the executive sponsor, and are they personally on the hook?
- For each perspective, what are the 1–3 objectives that actually move the strategy?
- Are our leading indicators things we can _change_, or just easy-to-measure noise?
- How will this scorecard cascade down — and who owns alignment at each level?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Telco company** (Pereira's worked example, page 44) — Financial: +5% net profit; Customer: +2.5% CSI; Internal: 90% UX score; Learning & Growth: 95% employee development plans completed
- **Mobil North American Marketing & Refining** — Kaplan & Norton's most-cited turnaround case, used throughout _The Balanced Scorecard_
- **CIGNA Property & Casualty** — cited in _The Strategy-Focused Organization_ (2000) for using BSC to climb from bottom-quartile to top-quartile performance
- **Wells Fargo Online Financial Services** — early BSC adopter in the financial services sector
- **Public-sector adoption** — government agencies, charities, sports teams; Pereira: now used worldwide (page 18)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[The Four Perspectives of BSC]] — _decomposes_into_ (the four pillars)
- [[Strategy Map]] — _visualized_by_ (the cause-and-effect diagram of BSC objectives)
- [[Cascading Balanced Scorecards]] — _operationalized_by_ (org-to-team alignment mechanism)
- [[Building a Balanced Scorecard]] — _see_also_ (Kaplan-Norton 8-step build process)
- [[BSC vs OKR]] — _see_also_ (when to choose which framework)
- [[BSC Anti-Patterns]] — _see_also_ (KPI overload, metric paralysis)
- [[Strategic Planning]] — _amplifies_
sections.Sources: - [[_Sources/Balanced_Scorecard-nxq9sa]] — pages 10–18 (definition, history, 1992 HBR origin, evolution), 21–24 (four perspectives), 28–36 (key features and implementation), 41–43 (benefits), 60–61 (criticisms and failure modes)

title: Bandwagon Effect
definition: The bandwagon effect is the tendency to adopt beliefs or behaviors simply because many others are doing so. Driven by the human need for social acceptance and the use of "social proof" as a decision-making heuristic — "if so many people believe it, it must be true" — it operates both consciously and unconsciously. Pereira singles out social-media platforms as the primary modern amplifier: likes, shares, follower counts, and algorithmic personalization create echo chambers where the bandwagon thrives, and the visibility of mass adoption becomes self-reinforcing evidence.

sections.Definition: The bandwagon effect is the tendency to adopt beliefs or behaviors simply because many others are doing so. Driven by the human need for social acceptance and the use of "social proof" as a decision-making heuristic — "if so many people believe it, it must be true" — it operates both consciously and unconsciously. Pereira singles out social-media platforms as the primary modern amplifier: likes, shares, follower counts, and algorithmic personalization create echo chambers where the bandwagon thrives, and the visibility of mass adoption becomes self-reinforcing evidence.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Social proof as heuristic** — the bigger the crowd, the stronger the cue
- **Conformity pressure** — fear of being excluded, ridiculed, or "left behind"
- **Operates consciously and unconsciously** — even people who pride themselves on independent thinking succumb
- **Algorithmically amplified** — personalization creates echo chambers where bandwagon thrives
- **Interacts with availability and confirmation** — popular ideas become more available and more confirmed
- **Self-reinforcing** — adoption signals to others, increasing adoption
- **Cross-domain** — politics, consumer trends, investment manias, technology adoption, fashion
sections.When you're most at risk:
- The decision involves genuine uncertainty and you're looking for cues
- You're consuming a personalized feed
- The trend has a quantitative popularity signal (likes, followers, downloads, market cap)
- The peer group around you has visibly adopted
- The cost of being out of step is socially salient (career, reputation)
- You're in a hot market (crypto, AI, IPO window, a specific design trend)
sections.When it's NOT a problem:
- The "crowd" is informed expert consensus (vaccinate your kids; the earth is round)
- Coordination problems where adopting the standard matters more than picking the optimal one (language, file formats, network protocols)
- Network-effect goods where value really does scale with adoption
- Following established best practices in domains where your expertise is shallow
sections.Common manifestations in business:
- **Investment manias** — dot-com, crypto, NFTs, meme stocks; capital chases what's hot (p.55)
- **Startup pattern matching** — VCs funding the "X for Y" of a hot model
- **Tech-stack fads** — engineers adopting frameworks because everyone is, not because of fit
- **Strategy fads** — "we need an AI strategy" with no underlying customer rationale
- **Hiring** — chasing candidates with offers from hot companies; ignoring quieter signals
- **Product roadmap** — building what competitors built because they built it
- **Cancel culture / pile-ons** — collective behavior cascading on minimal evidence
- **Election polling shifts** — undecideds breaking toward the perceived leader (p.56)
- **Influencer marketing** — engineered social-proof cascades (p.56)
sections.Indicators you're falling into it:
- Your justification for the choice references how many other people are doing it
- You feel social pressure to participate or be "left out"
- You can't articulate the first-principles reason to adopt
- You discovered the trend through your feed rather than from work
- The crowd is visibly larger than your evidence base
- You'd be embarrassed to publicly bet against it
sections.Countermeasures:
- **First-principles check** — would this be the right call if you'd never heard of the trend?
- **Demand the underlying evidence** — popularity is not evidence
- **Diversify your information diet** — break out of the algorithmic echo chamber
- **Cultivate dissenting peers** — surround yourself with at least some people who push back
- **Slow down** — bandwagon spikes under FOMO pressure; sleep on it
- **Run a pre-mortem** — what's the failure mode if the crowd is wrong?
- **Look for survivorship distortion** — the loud cases are the survivors; how many quiet failures?
- **Distinguish coordination from belief** — adopt the standard if coordination is the point; doubt the belief if the cascade is the only evidence
- **Media literacy** — recognize manufactured consensus, manufactured virality
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Dot-com bubble** — capital and talent piled into anything ".com" (p.55)
- **Tulip mania (1637)** — original case study
- **2021 NFT and crypto cycle** — celebrity endorsement cascades drove retail buyers in at the top
- **GameStop / WallStreetBets** — social-coordination-driven price action
- **"Quiet quitting", "vibe coding", every viral business meme** — adopted before defined
- **WeWork at peak hype** — capital chasing a brand-led narrative
- **Beanie Babies, Pet Rocks, Fidget Spinners** — short-cycle consumer manias
- **Theranos** — investor cascade despite scientific skepticism
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Availability Heuristic]] — _amplifies_ (popular = highly available)
- [[Confirmation Bias]] — _amplifies_ (popularity reads as confirmation)
- [[Survivorship Bias]] — _see_also_ (loud successes mask quiet failures)
- [[Overconfidence Effect]] — _see_also_ (the crowd's confidence is contagious)
- [[Status Quo Bias]] — _see_also_ (once you're on the bandwagon, getting off is costly)
sections.Sources: - [[_Sources/Cognitive_Biases-uphx4y]] — pages 54-61 (mechanism, social-media amplification, navigation strategies)

title: BCG Matrix
definition: The BCG Matrix (Growth-Share Matrix) is a portfolio management framework developed by the Boston Consulting Group that classifies business units or products into four categories based on two dimensions: relative market share (competitive strength) and market growth rate (industry attractiveness). The four quadrants—Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, and Dogs—provide a strategic lens for resource allocation, investment decisions, and divestiture planning. Pereira presents the BCG Matrix as a practical tool for understanding where to invest, where to harvest, and where to exit, with clear action implications for each quadrant.

sections.Definition: The BCG Matrix (Growth-Share Matrix) is a portfolio management framework developed by the Boston Consulting Group that classifies business units or products into four categories based on two dimensions: relative market share (competitive strength) and market growth rate (industry attractiveness). The four quadrants—Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, and Dogs—provide a strategic lens for resource allocation, investment decisions, and divestiture planning. Pereira presents the BCG Matrix as a practical tool for understanding where to invest, where to harvest, and where to exit, with clear action implications for each quadrant.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Stars** (high growth, high share) — market leaders in fast-growing industries; require heavy investment to maintain position; future Cash Cows
- **Cash Cows** (low growth, high share) — market leaders in mature industries; generate more cash than they consume; fund Stars and Question Marks
- **Question Marks** (high growth, low share) — operate in attractive markets but lack competitive position; require strategic choice: invest heavily to become Stars, or divest
- **Dogs** (low growth, low share) — weak competitive position in unattractive markets; typically generate low profit or losses; candidates for divestiture or liquidation
sections.When to use:
- Multi-product company deciding where to allocate capital and talent
- Corporate strategy review to evaluate business unit portfolio health
- Venture capital or private equity portfolio review
- M&A target evaluation to understand which assets are stars vs. dogs
- Resource allocation when budget constraints force trade-offs
- Explaining to stakeholders why underperforming products are being sunset
sections.When NOT to use:
- Single-product startups with no portfolio to balance
- When market share is an irrelevant metric (e.g., network-effect platforms where engagement matters more)
- If market boundaries are ill-defined (e.g., "AI" is not a single market)
- When used as the sole decision tool—complement with customer data and operational metrics
- For short-term tactical decisions; BCG is a strategic, not operational, framework
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Defining markets too broadly** — a "Dog" in one segment may be a "Star" in a niche
- **Static analysis** — a Star can become a Dog if growth slows and share erodes; review annually
- **Ignoring synergies** — Dogs may support Stars through ecosystem effects or brand halo
- **Over-reliance on market share** — in innovation-driven markets, first-mover advantage can be fleeting
- **Harvesting Cash Cows too aggressively** — starves them of maintenance investment, accelerating decline
- **Treating all Question Marks the same** — some have a credible path to Star; others are hopeless
- **Divesting Dogs too quickly** — they may provide strategic cover or serve niche customers profitably
sections.Key metrics:
| Metric | How to measure | Use in BCG |
|---|---|---|
| **Relative Market Share** | Your share / Largest competitor's share | >1.0 = high share (left side of matrix) |
| **Market Growth Rate** | Annual growth % of the served market | >10% typically = high growth (top of matrix) |
| **Revenue Contribution** | Unit revenue / Total portfolio revenue | Identifies Cash Cows that fund the portfolio |
| **Profit Margin by Unit** | Unit profit / Unit revenue | Dogs often have negative or near-zero margins |
| **Investment Requirement** | Capex + R&D + Marketing for unit | Stars and Question Marks require the most |
sections.Decision questions:
- Which of our products/business units are Stars, and are we investing enough to maintain their trajectory?
- Which Cash Cows are we milking too hard, risking their decline?
- For each Question Mark, do we have a credible path to Star status within 2-3 years?
- Which Dogs are we keeping for emotional reasons rather than strategic logic?
- What percentage of our portfolio is in each quadrant, and is that balance sustainable?
- If we had to cut investment by 30%, which units would we protect and which would we sacrifice?
- Are we defining our markets correctly, or are we hiding Stars inside overly broad categories?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Apple** — iPhone = Cash Cow (mature market, dominant share); Apple Watch = Star (growing market, leading share); Apple TV+ = Question Mark (growing streaming market, low relative share); iPod = Dog (declining market, near-zero share, discontinued). This portfolio balance allows Apple to fund innovation from iPhone profits (Pereira, pp. 89–90)
- **Samsung** — smartphones = Star/Question Mark (growing market, strong but not dominant share); semiconductors = Cash Cow (mature market, dominant share); home appliances = Cash Cow (mature market, high share). Samsung uses semiconductor profits to fund smartphone R&D and marketing (Pereira, pp. 90–91)
- **General Electric (classic BCG case)** — historically used the matrix to manage a diverse industrial portfolio, divesting Dogs (small appliances) and investing in Stars (aircraft engines, medical devices). The framework became synonymous with GE's portfolio management discipline in the 1980s–90s (referenced in Pereira's broader strategy context)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Competitive Analysis]] — _requires_ (BCG is a portfolio-level competitive analysis tool)
- [[Porter's Five Forces]] — _see_also_ (analyzes industry attractiveness, which feeds into BCG's growth axis)
- [[Product Lifecycle]] — _see_also_ (Products move from Question Mark → Star → Cash Cow → Dog as they mature)
- [[Market Share]] — _see_also_ (the horizontal axis of the BCG Matrix)
- [[Divestiture Strategy]] — _see_also_ (the typical outcome for Dogs and some Question Marks)
sections.Sources: - [[Competitive_Analysis_Strategies_Pereira_2022]] — pages 77-93 (BCG Matrix definition, quadrant descriptions, strategic implications, examples with Apple and Samsung)

title: Blockbuster Business Model
definition: The Blockbuster Business Model concentrates a firm's resources on a small number of high-demand, high-volume products (the "hits"), capitalizing on Pareto-distributed demand: 20% of products typically drive 80% of sales. Named after blockbuster films (financially-successful popular movies), the strategy is the structural opposite of Long-Tail and was the dominant retail logic before e-commerce removed shelf-space constraints. Pereira's seven supporting factors: ease of adoption, reliability, limited shelf space, trend-following human nature, limited distribution channels, individuals wanting "the best," and profitability through hit + low-cost combination.

sections.Definition: The Blockbuster Business Model concentrates a firm's resources on a small number of high-demand, high-volume products (the "hits"), capitalizing on Pareto-distributed demand: 20% of products typically drive 80% of sales. Named after blockbuster films (financially-successful popular movies), the strategy is the structural opposite of Long-Tail and was the dominant retail logic before e-commerce removed shelf-space constraints. Pereira's seven supporting factors: ease of adoption, reliability, limited shelf space, trend-following human nature, limited distribution channels, individuals wanting "the best," and profitability through hit + low-cost combination.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Concentrated on hits** — top 20% of products get the marketing, shelf, and capital
- **Pareto-driven** — 80/20 distribution of revenue across products
- **Shelf-space efficient** — particularly fit for physical retail where shelves are finite
- **Trend-following** — relies on social dynamics where popular products gain more popularity
- **Lower analytical complexity** — bestseller data is easy to obtain and act on
- **Reliable in stable markets** — works well when category and consumer preferences are stable
- **Vulnerable to disruption** — long-tail competitors with infinite digital shelf space can erode
sections.When to use:
- Physical retail with constrained shelf space
- Categories with strong winner-take-most dynamics (movies, music, books historically)
- Mass-market consumer goods where economies of scale matter
- Stable categories with predictable bestseller patterns
- Resource-constrained firms that need focus to survive
sections.When NOT to use:
- Digital categories with infinite shelf space (long-tail wins)
- Niche markets where the hits are too few or too contested
- When customers actively want differentiation/customization
- Categories experiencing rapid fragmentation (e.g., entertainment post-streaming)
- When customer data enables personalization (long-tail becomes feasible)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Sticking to blockbuster strategy as digital removes shelf-space constraint
- Missing the long-tail revenue opportunity that aggregates to substantial amounts
- Over-investing in hit detection (most "predictable" hits aren't)
- Letting marketing spend concentrate on hits while ignoring niche growth
- Failing to evolve as new competitors aggregate the long tail
sections.Key metrics:
- Hit revenue share (% from top N% of products)
- Top 20% SKU revenue concentration (canonical Pareto signal)
- Marketing concentration (% of marketing on top 20% of products)
- Shelf-space efficiency (revenue per shelf-foot in physical retail)
- Hit detection accuracy (% of predicted hits that became actual hits)
sections.Decision questions:
- Is our category structurally Pareto, or is the distribution flattening?
- What's our hit-revenue concentration, and is it rising or falling?
- Are we missing long-tail revenue we could capture with low marginal cost?
- What does a long-tail competitor look like, and how would they attack our hits?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Traditional movie theaters** — blockbuster releases concentrated showtimes; Netflix disrupted via long tail (page 17)
- **Traditional bookstores** — bestseller-focused shelving; Amazon disrupted via infinite-catalog long tail
- **Mass-market retailers** (Walmart, Target) — top-SKU concentrated; balanced with broader catalog
- **Major label music** historically — hit-driven A&R; Spotify recommendation flattened this
- **Network TV** — blockbuster prime-time shows; cable + streaming fragmented audience
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Long-Tail Business Model]] — _contrasts_with_
- [[Pareto Principle]] — _amplifies_ (blockbuster is Pareto applied to product selection)
- [[Cost Leadership]] — _see_also_ (often combined: hit + low-cost)
- [[Digital Disruption]] — _see_also_ (digital removed shelf-space constraint that drove blockbuster)

title: Blue Ocean Strategy
definition: Blue Ocean Strategy (originally Kim & Mauborgne, 2005) is the deliberate creation of uncontested market space — a "blue ocean" — either by creating an entirely new market or by disrupting an existing one in a way that makes competition irrelevant. The framework contrasts with "red oceans" where firms fight over the same customers with similar offerings, eroding margins. The strategy works by combining cost reduction in some dimensions with differentiation in others, producing a "value innovation" that opens new demand. Pereira's example is Cirque du Soleil, which created a new market for circus performances by blending theater, dance, and acrobatics — sidestepping competition with traditional circuses entirely.

sections.Definition: Blue Ocean Strategy (originally Kim & Mauborgne, 2005) is the deliberate creation of uncontested market space — a "blue ocean" — either by creating an entirely new market or by disrupting an existing one in a way that makes competition irrelevant. The framework contrasts with "red oceans" where firms fight over the same customers with similar offerings, eroding margins. The strategy works by combining cost reduction in some dimensions with differentiation in others, producing a "value innovation" that opens new demand. Pereira's example is Cirque du Soleil, which created a new market for circus performances by blending theater, dance, and acrobatics — sidestepping competition with traditional circuses entirely.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Uncontested market space** — winning by avoiding the fight, not by winning it
- **Value innovation** — simultaneously reducing some dimensions of competition while raising others, breaking the cost-differentiation tradeoff
- **New demand creation** — attracts non-customers of the existing category, not just steals share
- **Time-limited advantage** — once successful, competitors flood in; the blue ocean turns red
- **Strategy canvas** — visual tool comparing offering's competitive dimensions against industry baseline
- **Eliminate-Reduce-Raise-Create grid** — practical tool for designing the value innovation
sections.When to use:
- When the industry is mature, commoditized, and margin-eroding
- When current customers are paying for features they don't actually value (eliminate opportunity)
- When non-customers exist who would buy a different offering altogether
- When the firm has the conviction and resources to create a category, not just enter one
- When competing head-to-head against incumbents is uneconomic
sections.When NOT to use:
- In emerging markets where the category itself is still being defined
- When the firm lacks the brand, distribution, or marketing reach to educate a new category
- When the "blue ocean" is actually too small to be a viable business
- When the cost of category creation exceeds the value of the eventual win
- When customers in the existing category are highly satisfied (no defection signal)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Declaring blue ocean while still competing on existing-category dimensions
- Underestimating the marketing investment needed to teach customers about a new category
- Confusing differentiation within a red ocean with true blue ocean
- Creating a blue ocean that's small enough to be ignored by incumbents (and never grows)
- Failing to defend the blue ocean once others copy — first-mover doesn't guarantee winner
sections.Key metrics:
- Revenue from customers new to the category (not just new to the firm)
- Time before first credible competitor enters
- Gross margin in the new space vs. parent industry
- Strategy canvas divergence (how different is our profile from incumbents')
- Customer awareness of the new category
sections.Decision questions:
- Are we competing in a red ocean disguised as a blue one? (Apply the strategy canvas.)
- Which competitive dimensions can we ELIMINATE that the industry takes for granted?
- Which can we REDUCE below the industry standard?
- Which should we RAISE well above it?
- Which CREATE that the industry has never offered?
- Are there non-customers we could attract by changing the offering's shape?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Cirque du Soleil** — created a new market for circus performances by combining elements of theater, dance, and acrobatics, sidestepping traditional circus competition entirely (page 18)
- **Airbnb** — created the home-sharing category, attracting travelers who wouldn't have stayed in hotels at all (page 41)
- **Uber** — created an alternative to traditional taxi-hailing with on-demand ride sharing (page 44)
- **Netflix** — streaming subscriptions opened a new market structure beyond traditional TV and movie rental (page 42)
- **Tesla** — premium electric vehicles redefined the boundary between automotive and tech (pages 25, 43)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Cost Leadership]] — _alternative_to_
- [[Differentiation Strategy]] — _alternative_to_ (blue ocean is differentiation taken to category creation)
- [[Niche Market Strategy]] — _see_also_ (niches can sometimes seed blue oceans)
- [[Innovation Strategy]] — _amplifies_ (innovation is often the engine of value innovation)
- [[Value Proposition]] — _requires_

title: Brand Identity
definition: Brand identity is the distinct, recognizable set of associations a firm builds in customers' minds — encompassing visual identity, voice, values, customer experience, and the cumulative impression of every interaction. Strong brand identity allows a firm to charge a premium, build loyalty, and resist competition that copies products but cannot copy meaning. Pereira returns to this concept repeatedly across examples: Nike's brand identity around innovation and athletic excellence, Apple's around design and innovation, Patagonia's around sustainability, Uniqlo's around simplicity, quality, and affordability — each built deliberately and consistently over years.

sections.Definition: Brand identity is the distinct, recognizable set of associations a firm builds in customers' minds — encompassing visual identity, voice, values, customer experience, and the cumulative impression of every interaction. Strong brand identity allows a firm to charge a premium, build loyalty, and resist competition that copies products but cannot copy meaning. Pereira returns to this concept repeatedly across examples: Nike's brand identity around innovation and athletic excellence, Apple's around design and innovation, Patagonia's around sustainability, Uniqlo's around simplicity, quality, and affordability — each built deliberately and consistently over years.
sections.Key attributes:
- **More than visuals** — brand identity is meaning, voice, values, behavior, and experience, not just logo and colors
- **Built through repetition** — sustained consistency across years and touchpoints, not one campaign
- **Customer-perceived** — what matters is the customer's mental association, not the firm's intent
- **Tied to a defining attribute** — strong brands stand for ONE clear thing (innovation, quality, ethics, simplicity)
- **Hard to copy** — products can be cloned; brand meaning takes a decade or more to build
- **Decays without investment** — brand identity erodes if not actively reinforced
sections.When to use:
- B2C markets where customers care about who they're buying from, not just what
- Premium positioning that needs to justify a price premium
- Markets where products are commoditizing — brand is what's left
- Niche segments where the brand IS the differentiator (Patagonia, Whole Foods)
- Talent acquisition (strong brands attract better hires)
sections.When NOT to use:
- As a substitute for product strength — brand can amplify a strong product but not rescue a weak one
- In pure commodity transactions where the customer has no recall and no preference
- When the firm changes positioning every quarter (kills the consistency that builds identity)
- For very small or early-stage firms where building distinct brand identity is premature
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Confusing brand with logo or campaigns
- Standing for too many things — diluted identity that's recognizable for nothing
- Inconsistent execution across channels (in-store says one thing, social another)
- Letting individual product extensions weaken the parent brand
- Underinvesting in brand because the ROI is long-term and hard to measure
sections.Key metrics:
- Unaided brand awareness in target segment
- Brand equity index (e.g., Interbrand, Kantar)
- NPS (correlated with brand strength)
- Customer loyalty / repeat purchase rate
- Price premium achievable vs. unbranded equivalents
- Share of voice in category conversation
sections.Decision questions:
- In one word: what does our brand stand for? Could we defend that to a customer?
- Is every customer touchpoint reinforcing that one word, or contradicting it?
- What does a competitor with a stronger brand do that we don't?
- Are we investing in brand systematically, or hoping it accumulates?
- What's the proof point in our customer experience that earns our brand promise?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Apple** — brand identity built around design, innovation, and quality; consistent across iPhone, iPad, MacBook, retail, and service touchpoints (pages 14, 23, 43)
- **Nike** — strong brand identity around innovation and athletic excellence; built through differentiated products for athletes and fitness enthusiasts (pages 15, 40)
- **Patagonia** — sustainability, environmental responsibility, and outdoor lifestyle as the defining identity; built through both products AND business practices (pages 41, 48)
- **Uniqlo** — simplicity, quality, and affordability as the brand identity; customer-centric value propositions enable the positioning (page 52)
- **Tesla** — innovation, sustainability, and high-performance EVs as the brand identity (pages 25, 43)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Differentiation Strategy]] — _amplifies_ (brand identity is often the differentiator)
- [[Value Proposition]] — _requires_ (brand must align with what the firm promises)
- [[Customer Experience]] — _amplifies_ (CX is the operational expression of brand)
- [[Niche Market Strategy]] — _see_also_ (niches often build brand identity faster)
- [[Sustainable Business Practices]] — _see_also_ (Patagonia: practices = brand)

title: BSC Anti-Patterns
definition: BSC anti-patterns are the recurring failure modes Pereira and Kaplan & Norton document across decades of BSC implementations: KPI overload, metric paralysis, perspective imbalance, template copying, weak leadership sponsorship, and gaming behavior driven by misaligned incentives. Pereira's source devotes pages 60–62 explicitly to criticisms and surfaces additional warnings throughout the implementation chapters. The pattern: BSC fails not because the framework is wrong, but because organizations dilute its discipline — measuring everything, copying others, or letting executives delegate ownership.

sections.Definition: BSC anti-patterns are the recurring failure modes Pereira and Kaplan & Norton document across decades of BSC implementations: KPI overload, metric paralysis, perspective imbalance, template copying, weak leadership sponsorship, and gaming behavior driven by misaligned incentives. Pereira's source devotes pages 60–62 explicitly to criticisms and surfaces additional warnings throughout the implementation chapters. The pattern: BSC fails not because the framework is wrong, but because organizations dilute its discipline — measuring everything, copying others, or letting executives delegate ownership.
sections.Key attributes:
- **KPI overload** — FranklinCovey citation: 3 goals → all achieved; 4–10 → one or two; 10+ → none (page 33)
- **Metric paralysis** — Pereira: "when too many metrics are monitored, progress is often stalled" (page 32)
- **All-lagging measurement** — over-indexing on what's easy to count (revenue, headcount) and ignoring leading drivers (page 33)
- **Treating perspectives as parallel silos** — Pereira: "fundamentally wrong"; perspectives form a causal stack (page 34)
- **Template copying** — Kaplan & Norton: scorecard "should be unique in the industry"; copying defeats the model (page 14, 16, 60)
- **Weak leadership sponsorship** — BSC needs all levels of management to support it; absent buy-in, it dies (page 60)
- **Data fatigue** — managers and teams resent the documentation overhead and stop feeding the system (page 61–62)
- **Compensation-coupled KPIs that induce gaming** — Pereira: "bad KPIs are ones that make your team think about how to cheat the system instead of how to improve the product" (page 50)
- **Treating BSC as a measurement system** rather than a strategic management system — Kaplan & Norton's repeated warning (page 16)
- **Operational metrics smuggled into the scorecard** — they belong in operational reporting, not strategic KPI lists (page 32: operational measures are "housekeeping," not KPIs)
sections.When to use (this anti-pattern lens):
- Auditing an existing BSC that has stopped driving behavior
- Diagnosing why a 6-month-old scorecard has become "wallpaper"
- Pre-mortem before launching a new BSC build
- Reviewing cascaded scorecards across business units for inflation
sections.When NOT to use:
- As an excuse not to build a BSC at all (the anti-patterns are avoidable)
- To dismiss BSC discipline in startups that genuinely need OKRs (use the right tool, don't strawman BSC)
sections.Common pitfalls (recursive — these traps:
- **KPI sprawl on cascaded scorecards** — each unit adds its own KPIs; total count balloons across the org
- **Selective perspective coverage** — units skip Learning & Growth ("HR's problem") or Customer (internal teams)
- **Stale reviews** — "blue book" review cadence (every 3–4 months, page 27) skipped; scorecard becomes ceremonial
- **Consultant-built, never owned** — scorecard dies the day external facilitators leave
- **Executive theater** — leaders attend the launch workshop, then disengage; middle management is left holding it
- **Tracking everything because dashboards make it easy** — modern BI tools enable, not prevent, KPI overload
sections.Key metrics (warning signals):
- **KPI count > 25 at corporate level** — high risk of overload
- **Perspective imbalance** — any perspective < 10% of objectives, or any > 50%
- **Leading-vs-lagging ratio skewed lagging** (< 30% leading) — Pereira's warning (page 33)
- **Review cadence compliance < 75%** — scorecard is dying
- **Executive workshop attendance dropping quarter-over-quarter** — sponsorship is eroding
- **Number of KPIs tied to compensation** — every such linkage is a gaming-incentive risk (page 50)
sections.Decision questions (diagnostic):
- How many KPIs are on our corporate scorecard? On our cascaded scorecards? (If totals exceed FranklinCovey's "rule of 3," cull)
- For each KPI, is it leading or lagging? Can we actually influence it?
- When did our executive sponsor last personally engage with the scorecard?
- Are we measuring this because it matters, or because the data is easy?
- Which KPIs are tied to bonus/comp — and are people gaming them?
- Did we copy our perspective structure from another company without customizing?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **"Kill another tree" program** — Pereira's quoted joke about one company's tendency to add policies/precautions in response to every consultant idea (page 12) — illustrates KPI/policy sprawl
- **Lagging-only strategic plans** — Pereira's observation that "most strategic plans focus on measures that are already behind schedule" because they're easy to count (page 33)
- **Weight-loss analogy** (page 33) — getting on the scale (lagging) tells you the result but does nothing to change it; tracking runs and meals (leading) is what actually moves the outcome
- **Project KPI gaming** — Pereira's warning against tying 100%-on-time delivery to compensation: incentivizes corner-cutting on quality (page 50)
- **Generic-template adoption failures** — Kaplan & Norton's repeated case studies of firms that copied another company's BSC and saw no behavior change
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Balanced Scorecard]] — _harms_ (these anti-patterns degrade the framework)
- [[Building a Balanced Scorecard]] — _see_also_ (the build process is designed to prevent many of these)
- [[Cascading Balanced Scorecards]] — _see_also_ (cascading is where KPI overload typically explodes)
- [[The Four Perspectives of BSC]] — _see_also_ (perspective imbalance is a top anti-pattern)
- [[BSC vs OKR]] — _see_also_ (many of these anti-patterns apply to OKRs as well)
sections.Sources: - [[_Sources/Balanced_Scorecard-nxq9sa]] — pages 12–13 (BSC misuse and "kill another tree" sprawl), 16 (Kaplan & Norton's warning against generic templates and measurement-only framing), 32–34 (metric paralysis, focus discipline, leading vs lagging), 50 (compensation-coupled KPIs and gaming), 60–62 (formal criticisms: customization burden, leadership buy-in, complexity, data overhead)

title: BSC vs OKR
aliases: Scorecard vs Objectives and Key Results
definition: The Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton, 1992) and Objectives & Key Results (originated at Intel by Andy Grove, popularized by Google via John Doerr) are the two dominant strategy-execution frameworks of the last 35 years. Both translate strategy into measurable goals, but they differ on time horizon, organizational scope, structural rigidity, and where they fit on a company's maturity curve. The Balanced Scorecard is a multi-year management system organized around four causal perspectives; OKRs are a quarterly goal-setting cadence with no fixed perspective structure. Pereira's BSC source establishes the BSC side of this comparison; OKR descriptions here reflect the canonical Doerr/Google framing.

sections.Definition: The Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton, 1992) and Objectives & Key Results (originated at Intel by Andy Grove, popularized by Google via John Doerr) are the two dominant strategy-execution frameworks of the last 35 years. Both translate strategy into measurable goals, but they differ on time horizon, organizational scope, structural rigidity, and where they fit on a company's maturity curve. The Balanced Scorecard is a multi-year management system organized around four causal perspectives; OKRs are a quarterly goal-setting cadence with no fixed perspective structure. Pereira's BSC source establishes the BSC side of this comparison; OKR descriptions here reflect the canonical Doerr/Google framing.
sections.Key attributes:
- **BSC: multi-year management system** — Pereira: not a tool that can be "finished in a few weeks or months" (page 13)
- **OKR: quarterly goal-setting cadence** — designed for 90-day cycles with mid-quarter check-ins
- **BSC: four prescribed perspectives** — Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth (page 21)
- **OKR: no prescribed perspectives** — objectives are free-form, structured only as Objective + 3–5 Key Results
- **BSC: top-down + cascading** — corporate scorecard cascades to units, then teams, then individuals (page 40)
- **OKR: bidirectional** — ~60% bottom-up, ~40% top-down in Google's original practice
- **BSC: leading + lagging KPIs explicitly balanced** — Pereira: most strategic plans over-index on lagging (page 33)
- **OKR: Key Results are typically outcome-oriented** — closer to lagging, with separate KPIs for leading indicators
- **BSC: tied to formal strategic planning** — annual strategy refresh, quarterly reviews (page 27)
- **OKR: tied to operational cadence** — quarterly setting, weekly/biweekly check-ins
- **BSC: built for large orgs** — requires CEO sponsorship, facilitators, multiple workshops (page 22, 25–27)
- **OKR: built for fast-moving teams** — Andy Grove designed it at Intel for engineering velocity
sections.When to use:
- **Use BSC when** the org has a stable multi-year strategy, multiple business units that need alignment, formal board/investor reporting requirements, public-sector accountability obligations, or a mature management culture that can absorb the build process
- **Use OKRs when** the org is in a high-uncertainty phase (startup, scaleup, pivot), strategy is emergent and refined quarterly, teams have meaningful autonomy and ambition matters more than predictability, or the executive team values speed over comprehensive coverage
- **Use both** when the BSC supplies the multi-year strategic frame and quarterly OKRs operationalize a slice of it (common in mature enterprises with innovation arms)
sections.When NOT to use:
- **Do not use BSC** in early-stage startups still discovering product-market fit (too heavyweight, page 60–62)
- **Do not use OKRs** as your sole strategy framework in a large regulated enterprise where multi-year financial accountability matters
- **Do not stack both** without clear seams; double-reporting kills both systems
- **Do not adopt either** without executive sponsorship — both fail the same way when leadership disengages
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Treating them as interchangeable** — they answer different questions (BSC: "is our strategy on track across all dimensions?"; OKR: "what is the team ambitiously trying to ship this quarter?")
- **Forcing the BSC's four perspectives onto OKRs** — collapses OKR's flexibility without gaining BSC's causal structure
- **Running OKRs at BSC cadence** (annual) — loses the velocity advantage
- **Running BSC at OKR cadence** (quarterly resets) — destroys the multi-year strategic story
- **Tying compensation to OKR Key Results** — the original Google practice explicitly decouples them; Pereira's BSC warning on KPIs and cheating applies equally (page 50)
- **Adopting either as a vanity exercise** — both die without engaged executive sponsorship
sections.Key metrics:
- **Strategy execution rate** — % of scorecard objectives / OKRs on track
- **KPI alignment score** — % of unit metrics laddering to corporate strategy (BSC) or objectives (OKR)
- **Cadence fit** — does the framework's cycle match the organization's pace of change?
- **Engagement rate** — % of leaders/teams actively using the system vs treating it as overhead
sections.Decision questions:
- What is our planning horizon — 3–5 years (BSC) or 1–4 quarters (OKR)?
- How predictable is our strategy? (Stable → BSC; emergent → OKR)
- Do we have board/regulatory accountability that requires comprehensive perspective coverage?
- Can our executives commit to the BSC's multi-workshop build, or do we need something teams can adopt next quarter?
- Are we trying to align a large multi-unit org (BSC strength) or accelerate ambitious team velocity (OKR strength)?
- Do we already have one framework? If so, what gap is the other supposed to fill?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **BSC: Mobil NAM&R, CIGNA, Wells Fargo** — Kaplan & Norton's foundational enterprise cases
- **BSC: Telco worked example** (Pereira, page 44) — four perspectives, four objectives, four KPIs, four initiatives
- **OKR: Intel under Andy Grove** — the original implementation
- **OKR: Google** — adopted in 1999, scaled with company; still core today
- **Both: mature enterprises** — common pattern is corporate BSC providing 3-year strategy frame, with product/engineering orgs running quarterly OKRs against BSC objectives
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Balanced Scorecard]] — _compares_with_
- [[OKR Framework]] — _see_also_
- [[Strategy Map]] — _see_also_ (the BSC's primary differentiator from OKRs)
- [[Cascading Balanced Scorecards]] — _see_also_ (BSC's alignment mechanism; OKRs use a different bidirectional pattern)
- [[BSC Anti-Patterns]] — _see_also_ (many anti-patterns apply to both frameworks)
sections.Sources: - [[_Sources/Balanced_Scorecard-nxq9sa]] — pages 10–13 (what BSC is and is not), 16 (Kaplan & Norton's warning against generic templates), 21 (four perspectives as BSC's structural signature), 33–35 (leading vs lagging discipline), 60–62 (BSC's failure modes — many shared with OKRs). OKR characterizations here reflect canonical Doerr/Grove framing, not the Pereira source.

title: Build a Community of Fans
definition: This is Pereira's design principle for the Customer Relationships block of any exponential business: rather than acquiring "users" one at a time, design the product so that customers become active participants who co-create value, customize the experience, and bring others in. The mechanism is to give users meaningful control over the product's expression — filters, lenses, mashups, playlists, profiles — so that personal expression becomes the seed of viral distribution. Each customer becomes a brand ambassador as a side effect of using the product, not as a separate ambassador program. The deeper claim is that "community" is not a marketing channel; it is a value-production mode that turns retention, virality, and word-of-mouth into structural properties of the business rather than line items in the marketing budget.

sections.Definition: This is Pereira's design principle for the Customer Relationships block of any exponential business: rather than acquiring "users" one at a time, design the product so that customers become active participants who co-create value, customize the experience, and bring others in. The mechanism is to give users meaningful control over the product's expression — filters, lenses, mashups, playlists, profiles — so that personal expression becomes the seed of viral distribution. Each customer becomes a brand ambassador as a side effect of using the product, not as a separate ambassador program. The deeper claim is that "community" is not a marketing channel; it is a value-production mode that turns retention, virality, and word-of-mouth into structural properties of the business rather than line items in the marketing budget.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Users as co-producers** — features that let customers create something they want to show off (Instagram filters, Snap lenses, Musical.ly mashups) (page 37)
- **Personal expression as viral distribution** — every share is a free acquisition channel (page 37)
- **Belonging as a retention force** — fans stay because they feel part of something, not just because the product is good (page 37)
- **Product becomes part of identity and routine** — daily use of customizable features deepens identity attachment (page 37)
- **Community is the flywheel seed** — Pereira's implementation prescription: "Creating a community is the first step in implementing flywheel concepts in your organization" (page 87)
- **Ambassador programs as accelerant, not substitute** — formal ambassadors amplify but cannot replace organic community
- **Customization tools as the primary investment** — the leverage is in giving fans creative power, not in talking at them
- **Two-way value** — communities need to receive something (status, tools, voice), not just be harvested
sections.When to use:
- The product has emotional or expressive surface area (creative tools, content platforms, lifestyle brands)
- You compete in a category where consumer acquisition costs are high and word-of-mouth is structurally cheap
- The customer base is large and socially connected enough that referral compounding clears meaningful volume
- The business model can sustain investment in community before monetization (long horizon)
- You are designing a platform or ecosystem and need supply-side enthusiasm as well as demand-side advocacy
sections.When NOT to use:
- The product is purely functional with no expressive surface area (B2B utilities, infrastructure)
- The customer base is too small or too dispersed for community dynamics to compound
- You cannot resource the community operations (moderation, tools, events, content)
- The category is sensitive and customers actively want privacy or low-profile use (security, healthcare)
- The brand cannot tolerate community-driven content (regulated industries, fragile brand contexts)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **"Community" without community management** — opening a Discord and walking away
- **Harvesting without giving** — using the community for free marketing without providing tools, status, or revenue
- **Confusing audience with community** — followers consume; community members create
- **Toxic community drift** — moderation neglect produces communities that drive customers away rather than in
- **Over-controlling expression** — fans use products that give them creative freedom; locked-down "branded" expression dies
- **Ambassador programs without organic seed** — paid ambassadors layered on a non-community produce hollow advocacy
- **Confusing community with customer success** — both matter; they are not the same function
sections.Key metrics:
- **User-generated content share** — % of in-product content created by customers vs. brand
- **Referral rate** — % of new customers attributable to existing customers
- **Community engagement** — active community members / total customers
- **Time spent in community surfaces** vs. time spent in product
- **Brand advocacy / NPS** — willingness to recommend (proxy for fan status)
- **Customization usage rate** — % of users who exercise expressive features
- **Organic-vs-paid mix** — share of new users from non-paid channels (rises as community matures)
sections.Decision questions:
- What is the smallest meaningful piece of creative control we can give customers?
- Are we acquiring users or recruiting fans — and what's the difference in our funnel?
- Who in the org owns community as a function, and is that role resourced to succeed?
- What do we give the community that they could not get for free elsewhere?
- Are our customization features being used, or are they fancy buttons no one presses?
- If our community went silent for a quarter, would we notice — and what would break first?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Instagram filters** — every shared photo carries the Instagram aesthetic and acts as an ambassador (page 37)
- **Snap lenses** — augmented-reality face filters create personal expression and viral content (page 37)
- **Musical.ly mashup functionality** — user-generated content fundamental to the platform's growth (page 37)
- **Waze contributors** — community-supplied road and traffic data, $1.3B exit at 100 employees (page 38)
- **Spotify playlists** — user-curated public playlists turn listeners into curators
- **GitHub** — developer community is the platform's gravity, $7.5B acquisition by Microsoft (page 49)
- **Reddit / Discord communities** — moderator-led communities that hold platforms together
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Network Effects]] — _amplifies_
- [[Flywheel Business Model]] — _complements_ (community is the flywheel seed)
- [[Customer Feedback Loop]] — _see_also_
- [[Exponential Business Model]] — _operationalizes_
- [[Nine Design Principles for Exponential Transformation]] — _see_also_ (principle 3)
- [[Loyalty Program]] — _see_also_
- [[Customer Relationships]] — _see_also_

related.title: Nine Design Principles for Exponential Transformation

title: Build-Measure-Learn Loop
definition: Build-Measure-Learn (B.M.L.) is the iterative feedback loop at the heart of Lean Startup. You **build** the smallest thing that tests a hypothesis (typically an MVP), **measure** how real users respond using actionable metrics, and **learn** whether the hypothesis is validated, falsified, or unclear — then start the cycle again with the next-most-risky assumption. Pereira describes it as "one of the most important steps in building an MVP," because it converts feedback into a continuous improvement engine rather than a one-time launch event. The goal is to minimize the time through the loop — faster cycles mean more validated learning per dollar.

sections.Definition: Build-Measure-Learn (B.M.L.) is the iterative feedback loop at the heart of Lean Startup. You **build** the smallest thing that tests a hypothesis (typically an MVP), **measure** how real users respond using actionable metrics, and **learn** whether the hypothesis is validated, falsified, or unclear — then start the cycle again with the next-most-risky assumption. Pereira describes it as "one of the most important steps in building an MVP," because it converts feedback into a continuous improvement engine rather than a one-time launch event. The goal is to minimize the time through the loop — faster cycles mean more validated learning per dollar.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Three-step cycle** — Build → Measure → Learn → (repeat with adjusted hypothesis)
- **Hypothesis-driven** — each cycle tests a stated assumption, not a vague intuition
- **Speed over polish** — cycle time is the key constraint; faster loops compound
- **Actionable metrics required** — vanity metrics (followers, downloads) break the loop
- **Feedback is the input, not the output** — the *learning* informs the next Build
- **Two valid outcomes** — persevere (current direction is working) or pivot (change direction)
- **Pairs naturally with Lean UX** — which uses Prototype-Measure-Learn variant (page 109)
sections.When to use:
- Anytime you're building a product whose market reception is uncertain
- Whenever a new feature represents a real hypothesis (not a known-needed fix)
- During the post-MVP scale-up phase, to validate each addition before fully building
- In rapidly changing markets where customer needs shift faster than annual roadmaps
- For any "innovation accounting" — when leadership needs to track learning, not just shipping
sections.When NOT to use:
- Pure execution work where the hypothesis is already validated (just ship it)
- Compliance or regulatory features (you don't get to "measure" whether to follow law)
- Internal tooling where the user IS the team and feedback is continuous anyway
- When the cycle time exceeds the strategic window (sometimes you have to commit)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Measuring vanity metrics** — Twitter followers don't tell you the product works (page 52)
- **Building too much per cycle** — turns a learning loop into waterfall with extra steps
- **Skipping the Learn step** — teams build, measure, and immediately re-build without synthesis
- **Confirmation bias in Learn** — interpreting ambiguous results as validation
- **Not defining the success/failure threshold in advance** — moving goalposts after data arrives
- **Loop time longer than market response time** — competitors out-iterate you
- **Treating BML as a project methodology** — it's a feedback discipline; agile/scrum is orthogonal
sections.Key metrics:
- **Cycle time** — Build → Measure → Learn → next Build duration
- **Validated learning per cycle** — quantum of hypothesis-clearing per loop
- **Hypothesis validation rate** — % of cycles that produce a clear yes/no
- **Pivot decisions made** — count of strategic changes triggered by data
- **Actionable metric movement** — conversion, retention, activation, etc.
sections.Decision questions:
- What is the riskiest assumption we should test this cycle?
- What's the cheapest way to build something that tests that assumption?
- What metric — measurable in days, not months — would falsify the hypothesis?
- What's our pre-committed threshold for "validated" vs "falsified"?
- Did the previous cycle's learning actually change what we're building, or did we ignore it?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Spotify** — relentless build-measure-learn iteration powered its growth from 2006 startup to $23B+ valuation; user feedback shaped every release (page 106)
- **Dropbox** — explainer-video MVP measured demand → 70,000 signups → learned the value prop resonated → built the product (pages 17, 81)
- **Foursquare** — launched with check-ins only; measured engagement; learned what users wanted next; iterated to add recommendations and city guides (page 79)
- **Buffer** — landing-page MVP → measured signups and tweet feedback → learned the offer worked → built the tool (page 80)
- **Instagram** — released photo+filter+share core (Oct 2010) → 24k users in 24 hours → learned demand was real → expanded over years to video, DMs, Stories (page 107)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Lean Startup Methodology]] — _specializes_ (BML is the core loop)
- [[Minimum Viable Product]] — _amplifies_ (MVPs are what you Build in the loop)
- [[Validated Learning]] — _see_also_ (the output of the Learn step)
- [[Vanity Metrics vs Actionable Metrics]] — _see_also_ (must use actionable to make the loop work)

title: Building a Balanced Scorecard
definition: Building a Balanced Scorecard is the structured 8-step process Kaplan and Norton documented for going from "we want a BSC" to "we have a live, cascaded scorecard." Pereira presents two complementary process views: the 8-step "typical project profile" (Preparation → 3 interview/workshop rounds → Implementation → Periodic Reviews, pages 25–27) and a 9-step usage cycle (Assessment → Strategy → Strategic Objectives → Strategy Mapping → Performance Measures → Strategic Initiatives → Performance Analysis → Alignment → Evaluation, pages 37–40). Both versions share the same core: heavy upfront executive engagement, iterative drafting through interviews and workshops, then continuous review.

sections.Definition: Building a Balanced Scorecard is the structured 8-step process Kaplan and Norton documented for going from "we want a BSC" to "we have a live, cascaded scorecard." Pereira presents two complementary process views: the 8-step "typical project profile" (Preparation → 3 interview/workshop rounds → Implementation → Periodic Reviews, pages 25–27) and a 9-step usage cycle (Assessment → Strategy → Strategic Objectives → Strategy Mapping → Performance Measures → Strategic Initiatives → Performance Analysis → Alignment → Evaluation, pages 37–40). Both versions share the same core: heavy upfront executive engagement, iterative drafting through interviews and workshops, then continuous review.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Eight project steps** — Preparation, 1st Interviews, 1st Workshop, 2nd Interviews, 2nd Workshop, 3rd Workshop, Implementation, Periodic Reviews (page 25–27)
- **Nine usage steps** — Assessment, Strategy, Strategic Objectives, Strategy Mapping, Performance Measures, Strategic Initiatives, Performance Analysis, Alignment, Evaluation (page 37–40)
- **Heavy executive interview load** — facilitator works 90 minutes with each senior manager in round 1 (page 26)
- **Three workshops, not one** — successive workshops drive convergence: vision → measures → stretch targets → implementation plan
- **Bottom-up build, top-down review** — Pereira: start at Learning & Growth, work up to Financial (page 34)
- **Periodic review cadence** — "blue book" reviewed every 3–4 months; strategic refresh annually (page 27)
- **Facilitator role is structural** — synthesizes interview data, drafts scorecard, owns the workshop arc (page 26)
- **Implementation is its own project** — connects KPIs to databases and IT systems; not just a strategy exercise (page 27)
sections.When to use:
- Standing up a BSC for the first time in an organization or business unit
- Refreshing a stale BSC after a strategy shift, M&A, or leadership change
- When the team needs a structured cadence to force executive convergence on strategy
- When the BSC will be the formal management system, not an experimental dashboard
sections.When NOT to use:
- For a one-team dashboard (too heavyweight; use OKRs or a simple KPI tree)
- When executive calendars cannot absorb the workshop load
- Without a facilitator (internal or external) who owns synthesis between sessions
- If the strategy itself is undefined — Pereira: Assessment step requires existing mission/vision/values to validate (page 37)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Skipping the interview rounds** — workshops without prep produce shallow consensus
- **One workshop instead of three** — Pereira's process explicitly uses three to drive convergence and stretch (page 26–27)
- **No facilitator** — without someone synthesizing between sessions, scorecard drifts
- **Implementation step underestimated** — wiring KPIs to data systems and IT is its own project (page 27)
- **No periodic review** — without the 3–4 month "blue book" cadence, the scorecard dies (page 27)
- **Treating Assessment as optional** — skipping the current-state diagnosis means the scorecard does not address real problems (page 37)
- **Building scorecard without strategic themes** — themes (operational excellence, customer intimacy) are how the strategy clusters objectives (page 38)
- **No Strategic Initiatives** — defining KPIs without the projects that move them produces measurement theater (page 39)
sections.Key metrics:
- **Time to first scorecard** — weeks from kickoff to signed-off v1 (typical: 12–16 weeks)
- **Executive engagement hours** — total hours of senior-manager time invested
- **Strategic objective count** — should land at 10–20 for corporate level (FranklinCovey: 10+ goals = none achieved, page 33)
- **KPI count** — should land at 15–25 total (1–2 per objective, mix of leading and lagging)
- **Review cadence compliance** — % of scheduled 3–4 month reviews actually held
sections.Decision questions:
- Who is the executive sponsor, and will they personally attend all three workshops?
- Who is our facilitator (internal vs external), and what is their authority?
- What is our Assessment input — do we have validated mission/vision/values/market analysis?
- What 3–4 strategic themes will organize our objectives?
- Are we resourcing the Implementation step (IT integration) as its own workstream?
- What is our quarterly review cadence and who runs it?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Kaplan & Norton's "typical project profile"** — the 8-step build is documented in _The Balanced Scorecard_ (1996) and _The Strategy-Focused Organization_ (2000)
- **Telco BSC build** (Pereira, page 44) — illustrates output of completed build: four objectives × four perspectives, each with measure/KPI/initiative
- **Project Management scorecard build** (page 46) — applies the build process at the project level
- **HR scorecard build** (page 55) — applies the build process to a support function
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Balanced Scorecard]] — _operationalizes_ (this is the recipe)
- [[Strategy Map]] — _produces_ (strategy mapping is step 4 of the 9-step process)
- [[Cascading Balanced Scorecards]] — _see_also_ (cascading happens after corporate scorecard is built)
- [[BSC Anti-Patterns]] — _see_also_ (what goes wrong when the build process is skipped)
sections.Sources: - [[_Sources/Balanced_Scorecard-nxq9sa]] — pages 25–27 (8-step "typical project profile": Preparation through Periodic Reviews), 34–36 (right way vs wrong way to implement), 37–40 (9-step usage process: Assessment through Evaluation)

title: Bundle and Cross-Sell Pricing for Ecommerce
definition: Bundle and cross-sell pricing is the ecommerce tactic of grouping multiple products at a single price (bundle) or surfacing complementary items at the point of purchase to lift basket size (cross-sell). Pereira covers both forms: **bundle pricing** sells a combined package at a discount versus the sum of components (Amazon's "Frequently bought together"); **cross-sell** surfaces relevant attachments during browsing or checkout ("Customers who bought this also bought..."). The mechanism exploits the perception of bargain (bundle math feels generous), reduces decision friction (the attached item is already curated), and lifts AOV without paying additional acquisition cost. Amazon built this into a flywheel: algorithmic recommendation engines turn each visit into a higher-margin basket through cross-sell, even when individual items are loss-leaders. The discipline is in the bundle composition (must feel coherent) and the cross-sell relevance (must feel helpful, not pushy).

sections.Definition: Bundle and cross-sell pricing is the ecommerce tactic of grouping multiple products at a single price (bundle) or surfacing complementary items at the point of purchase to lift basket size (cross-sell). Pereira covers both forms: **bundle pricing** sells a combined package at a discount versus the sum of components (Amazon's "Frequently bought together"); **cross-sell** surfaces relevant attachments during browsing or checkout ("Customers who bought this also bought..."). The mechanism exploits the perception of bargain (bundle math feels generous), reduces decision friction (the attached item is already curated), and lifts AOV without paying additional acquisition cost. Amazon built this into a flywheel: algorithmic recommendation engines turn each visit into a higher-margin basket through cross-sell, even when individual items are loss-leaders. The discipline is in the bundle composition (must feel coherent) and the cross-sell relevance (must feel helpful, not pushy).
sections.Key attributes:
- **Bundle = single price for multiple items** — discounted vs. sum of parts to drive attach
- **Cross-sell = curated complementary suggestions** — at PDP, cart, or post-purchase
- **Upsell = same category, higher tier** — distinct from cross-sell but often stacked
- **Algorithmic engine** — "Customers who bought this also bought" personalizes attachments
- **AOV lift** — primary economic justification; doesn't require additional traffic spend
- **Bundle coherence** — items must feel naturally related (camera + memory card; not camera + socks)
- **Margin recovery** — cross-sell of high-margin attachments recovers loss-leader economics
- **Decision-easing** — reduces customer cognitive load on accessory choice
- **Post-purchase cross-sell** — order-confirmation page is high-intent real estate
sections.When to use:
- For products with natural attachments or accessories (camera + lens; console + games; sofa + throw)
- When you have an existing recommendation engine or algorithmic attach data
- To recover margin from loss-leader or competitively-priced anchor SKUs
- For categories with strong complementarity (fashion sets; meal kits; gift bundles)
- When you want to lift AOV without adding acquisition spend
- For checkout-page real estate that would otherwise be empty
sections.When NOT to use:
- When bundles feel forced (incoherent groupings damage trust)
- When the bundle discount cannibalizes higher-margin individual sales
- For commodity categories where customers shop one item at a time
- When the cross-sell load slows page-load speed (mobile conversion penalty)
- For premium / luxury items where bundle math feels cheapening
- When you don't have the data to make recommendations relevant
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Bundle discount > attach margin (you lose money on every bundle sold)
- Cross-sell that feels random or pushy degrades the shopping experience
- Bundles that include high-margin SKUs that would have sold solo at full price
- Failing to A/B test bundle composition (intuition is often wrong)
- Overcrowding the checkout page with cross-sell that slows conversion
- Treating cross-sell as ad inventory instead of merchandising (relevance > revenue)
- Building recommendation engines that optimize for clicks rather than basket profitability
sections.Key metrics:
- **Average Order Value (AOV)** — the headline lift metric
- **Attach rate** — % of orders containing >1 item
- **Items per order** — average basket depth
- **Bundle take rate** — % of customers who choose bundle over standalone item
- **Cross-sell conversion rate** — % of cross-sell impressions that convert
- **Gross margin per order** — must rise, not just AOV
- **Repeat purchase rate** of cross-sell-driven baskets
sections.Decision questions:
- What's the marginal economics of each bundle — are we adding profit or just AOV?
- Is our recommendation engine optimizing for relevance or for unrelated high-margin pushes?
- Where on the journey does cross-sell convert best (PDP, cart, post-purchase)?
- Are we testing bundle composition, or guessing?
- Are we measuring incremental basket lift, or counting cannibalized sales as wins?
- Does our cross-sell load fast enough on mobile?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Amazon "Frequently bought together"** — the canonical algorithmic bundle, drives major AOV lift (pages 122-123)
- **Amazon "Customers who bought this also bought"** — long-tail cross-sell across the entire catalog
- **Best Buy** — TV + soundbar + HDMI cable + extended warranty bundles at checkout
- **Apple** — AppleCare attach at every device purchase; iPhone + accessories upsell
- **Sephora** — gift bundles and "Build your routine" curated sets
- **HelloFresh** — meal-kit add-ons (dessert, breakfast) cross-sold at checkout
- **Shopify merchants** — bundle apps (Bundler, Bold Bundles) democratize the pattern for SMB
- **Steam / Epic Games** — game bundles, season passes, DLC cross-sell
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Loss-Leader Pricing for Ecommerce]] — _amplifies_ (cross-sell is the loss-leader margin recovery mechanism)
- [[Pricing Psychology]] — _see_also_ (bundle framing exploits perception of value)
- [[Bundling and Packaging Strategies]] — _see_also_ (the strategy-level concept)
- [[Promotional and Seasonal Pricing for Ecommerce]] — _see_also_ (bundles often deployed inside promotional events)
- [[Three-Tier Pricing]] — _see_also_ (related upsell mechanic for SaaS / subscription)

title: Bundle Pricing
definition: Bundle pricing sells multiple items together for a single price, usually at a discount to the sum of individual prices. Pereira distinguishes two structural variants: **pure bundling** (items sold only together — e.g., cable channel packages) and **mixed bundling** (items sold both bundled and individually, with the bundle priced attractively). Bundling captures consumer surplus, moves slow inventory by pairing it with bestsellers, and increases average transaction value — but at the cost of per-item margin.

sections.Definition: Bundle pricing sells multiple items together for a single price, usually at a discount to the sum of individual prices. Pereira distinguishes two structural variants: **pure bundling** (items sold only together — e.g., cable channel packages) and **mixed bundling** (items sold both bundled and individually, with the bundle priced attractively). Bundling captures consumer surplus, moves slow inventory by pairing it with bestsellers, and increases average transaction value — but at the cost of per-item margin.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Pure vs. mixed bundling** — only-bundled vs. bundle-or-individual
- **Discount vs. à la carte total** — perceived savings is the hook
- **Captures consumer surplus** — extracts value from cross-product willingness-to-pay
- **Moves slow inventory** — pair slow movers with bestsellers
- **Increases AOV** — higher transaction size, often higher total revenue
- **Erodes per-item margin** — total profit math must justify the discount
- **Simplifies decision** — fewer SKUs to evaluate vs. à la carte
sections.When to use:
- Complementary products with natural use together (camera + accessories)
- Slow-moving inventory you want to move alongside hot items
- Subscription tiers where breadth-of-feature is the differentiator
- Categories where customers find pricing complexity overwhelming
- When you want to defend against unbundled competitors
sections.When NOT to use:
- Customers want only one item — forcing the bundle alienates them
- Bundle obscures the perceived value of premium individual items
- Margins are already thin (bundle discount tips you negative)
- Bundle complexity confuses rather than simplifies
- When unbundling is the disruptive opportunity (cable cord-cutting)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Setting the bundle discount too deep — margin collapses
- Bundling items customers don't want — they refuse the bundle and buy nothing
- Pure bundling when mixed bundling would convert more
- Failing to communicate the savings clearly — perceived value evaporates
- Cannibalizing premium individual sales by bundling premium with low-tier
sections.Key metrics:
- **Average transaction value** (AOV)
- **Attach rate** (% of customers buying the bundle vs. à la carte)
- **Take rate** by bundle tier
- **Bundle margin** (after discount, after mix shift)
- **Units per transaction** (volume amplification)
- **Cannibalization rate** (loss of à la carte premium sales)
sections.Decision questions:
- Are the items in the bundle genuinely complementary, or just lumped?
- What discount level moves the bundle without crashing total margin?
- Should this be pure or mixed bundling for our category?
- Will the bundle cannibalize premium à la carte sales we'd otherwise capture?
- Can customers easily see the savings, or is the comparison obscured?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **McDonald's value meals** — burger + fries + drink at a meal-combo discount (page 198)
- **Adobe Creative Cloud** — multi-app bundle cheaper than individual app purchases (page 198)
- **Consumer electronics** — TV + warranty + accessories at a unified price (page 198)
- **Telecom triple-play** — internet + cable + phone bundled (page 199)
- **Microsoft Office** — Word + Excel + PowerPoint + more in one suite

- [[Decoy Pricing]] — _see_also_ (often combined: decoy + bundle)
- [[Three-Tier Pricing]] — _see_also_ (tiered bundles for SaaS)
- [[Pricing Mechanisms]] — _specializes_
- [[Psychological Pricing]] — _amplifies_ (perceived discount is psychological)
