title: ExO Canvas
definition: The ExO Canvas is a design framework that complements the Business Model Canvas (BMC) by forcing the team to plan how exponential leverage will be created — not just how value flows. Pereira presents it as the bridge between an MTP and a working business model: while the BMC captures customer segments, value propositions, channels, revenue streams, and the cost structure, the ExO Canvas captures the five external-leverage attributes that make a business *exponential* rather than merely well-designed. Used together, the two canvases ensure that a venture has both a coherent value-flow story (BMC) and a coherent leverage story (ExO Canvas). The author's prescription is iterative: define the BMC blocks, then validate each against the corresponding ExO attribute, and oscillate between the two until they reinforce each other.

sections.Definition: The ExO Canvas is a design framework that complements the Business Model Canvas (BMC) by forcing the team to plan how exponential leverage will be created — not just how value flows. Pereira presents it as the bridge between an MTP and a working business model: while the BMC captures customer segments, value propositions, channels, revenue streams, and the cost structure, the ExO Canvas captures the five external-leverage attributes that make a business *exponential* rather than merely well-designed. Used together, the two canvases ensure that a venture has both a coherent value-flow story (BMC) and a coherent leverage story (ExO Canvas). The author's prescription is iterative: define the BMC blocks, then validate each against the corresponding ExO attribute, and oscillate between the two until they reinforce each other.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Pairs with the Business Model Canvas** — neither canvas is sufficient alone (page 83)
- **Anchored on the MTP** — every attribute serves the Massive Transformative Purpose (page 81)
- **Five external-leverage attributes (SCALE)** — Staff on Demand, Community & Crowd, Algorithms, Leveraged Assets, Engagement (pages 84-85)
- **Asset-orchestration mindset** — leveraged assets are accessed, not owned (page 85)
- **Algorithmic substitution** — work currently done by partners/employees is reviewed for algorithmic replacement (page 85)
- **Engagement as a moat** — sustained relationships with customers, users, key partners, suppliers (page 85)
- **Iterative dialog with BMC** — define one, validate against the other, move back and forth (page 85)
- **Four-of-five rule of thumb** — using at least four ExO attributes (plus MTP) materially raises odds of exponential growth (page 83)
sections.When to use:
- Designing a new exponential venture from scratch (ExO from day one)
- Designing an "edge" business inside an established organization that is trying to escape its immune system
- Stress-testing an existing BMC to ask "where is our leverage?"
- Coaching a team through the difference between a *good* business and an *exponential* one
- Mapping competitors and asking which ExO attributes they exploit and which they ignore
sections.When NOT to use:
- The business is genuinely linear by design (artisanal craft, regulated specialist services) — applying ExO Canvas creates pressure to fake leverage that does not exist
- The team has not yet defined an MTP — without it, the canvas has nothing to orient toward and devolves into a checklist
- As a substitute for the BMC — it complements but does not replace the BMC; on its own it ignores cost structure and revenue logic
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Filling every box for completeness** — forcing all five attributes when only two genuinely apply produces a fictional model
- **Treating Staff on Demand as outsourcing** — it is a deeper architectural shift toward orchestrated talent, not a cost-cutting tactic
- **Confusing Community & Crowd with marketing** — community is a co-production force, not a distribution channel
- **Skipping the MTP** — the canvas becomes purely tactical without a purpose to organize the leverage around
- **Ignoring Engagement** — the easy attribute to under-resource; without it the other four leak value over time
- **Designing in isolation from the BMC** — leverage without a revenue model is innovation theater
sections.Key metrics:
- **Number of SCALE attributes meaningfully populated** — target ≥4 of 5 (page 83)
- **Share of operations algorithmically handled** — proxy for the Algorithms attribute
- **Asset orchestration ratio** — assets accessed via partners ÷ assets owned outright
- **Community contribution rate** — share of value or content created by non-employee community members
- **Engagement depth metric** — DAU/MAU, NPS, contribution per active user
sections.Decision questions:
- What is our MTP, and does every attribute on this canvas serve it?
- Which functions currently performed by employees or partners could become algorithmic instead?
- What assets are critical to our value proposition that we do not need to own?
- Who is our community, and what do they get from contributing — not just what we get from them?
- How do we measure engagement, and is it actually trending in the right direction?
- For each ExO attribute we leave blank, what is our honest answer for why?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Airbnb ExO Canvas** — referenced as the worked example throughout the design chapter (pages 80-83): MTP = belonging anywhere; Leveraged Assets = rooms owned by hosts; Community & Crowd = host/guest community; Algorithms = matching, pricing, ranking; Engagement = reviews, superhost status
- **Uber** — Staff on Demand (drivers), Algorithms (matching, surge pricing), Engagement (ratings) (pages 72-73)
- **Waze** — Community & Crowd at the core; entire dataset is user-contributed (page 38)
- **StitchFix** — Algorithms attribute extreme: 65+ data scientists driving almost every operational decision (page 40)
- **Zappos** — Networked Culture as an Engagement pattern (holacracy) (page 41)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Business Model Canvas]] — _complements_ (the two canvases are designed to be used in dialog)
- [[Exponential Business Model]] — _operationalizes_ (the canvas is how you design one)
- [[Massive Transformative Purpose]] — _anchored_by_ (without it the canvas has no center)
- [[Five Steps to Design an Exponential Business Model]] — _see_also_ (the process that wraps the canvas)
- [[Nine Design Principles for Exponential Transformation]] — _see_also_ (BMC-block-level guidance)

title: Explainer Video MVP
definition: An Explainer Video MVP is a validation tactic where you produce a short narrative video showing how a product *would* work — and measure demand based on how many viewers take action (sign up, request access, share). The product itself does not exist. The video does. Pereira's archetypal example is Dropbox: founders Drew Houston and Arash Ferdowsi couldn't easily explain file-sync to investors or users, so they made a 3-minute screencast showing the imagined experience. Posted to Hacker News, it drove 70,000 email signups in a single day — validation that funded development and proved the concept before any code shipped. The hypothesis under test is whether the *value proposition*, communicated clearly, attracts the right audience.

sections.Definition: An Explainer Video MVP is a validation tactic where you produce a short narrative video showing how a product *would* work — and measure demand based on how many viewers take action (sign up, request access, share). The product itself does not exist. The video does. Pereira's archetypal example is Dropbox: founders Drew Houston and Arash Ferdowsi couldn't easily explain file-sync to investors or users, so they made a 3-minute screencast showing the imagined experience. Posted to Hacker News, it drove 70,000 email signups in a single day — validation that funded development and proved the concept before any code shipped. The hypothesis under test is whether the *value proposition*, communicated clearly, attracts the right audience.
sections.Key attributes:
- **No working product required** — the video IS the experiment
- **Story-driven** — illustrates how the product solves a real problem in a narrative arc
- **Paired with email capture** — converts video viewers into a waitlist asset
- **Cheap and fast** — a screencast + voiceover can ship in days
- **Highly shareable** — works on Hacker News, Twitter/X, Reddit, Product Hunt
- **Tests value-prop clarity** — if the video can't convert, the proposition isn't sharp enough
- **Limitation: tests interest, not retention** — signups are not active users
sections.When to use:
- Products that are conceptually novel and hard to explain in text (file-sync, AI, new categories)
- Pre-build demand validation when engineering cost is high
- When the value proposition is visual or demonstrable
- For fundraising — investors respond to a clear narrative + traction signal
- To build a launch list before any code ships
- For products that require behavior change (video can show "before/after")
sections.When NOT to use:
- Products whose value is obvious in a screenshot or one-sentence pitch (just use a landing page)
- Categories where viewers are skeptical of video promises (B2B finance, regulated)
- When you can't produce a video that genuinely conveys the experience
- When making the video would cost more than building a single-feature MVP
- For incremental features within an existing product (use Fake Door instead)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Vanity-metric trap** — counting views instead of signups
- **Overproduction** — spending weeks on cinematography when a screencast would have sufficed
- **Promising things you can't build** — sets up disappointment and bad PR when you launch
- **No follow-up sequence** — the email list goes cold while you build
- **Wrong distribution channel** — video on the wrong platform produces no signal
- **Conflating viral hit with product-market fit** — a great video drives novelty signups, not necessarily product demand
- **Building exactly what the video showed** — when the right product, post-feedback, would differ
sections.Key metrics:
- Email signups (absolute and rate)
- Signup conversion rate (signups / unique viewers)
- Video completion rate (% who watch to the end)
- Cost per signup (if running paid distribution)
- Share-through rate (how viral)
- Geo / segment breakdown of signups (who actually resonated)
sections.Decision questions:
- Is our product novel enough that a video is the cheapest way to convey it?
- What conversion threshold, pre-committed, would we call "validated"?
- Do we have a follow-up sequence ready for the email list?
- Are we testing the value prop — or just our video-making skills?
- What's our plan if the video drives 70,000 signups (capacity, communication, prioritization)?
- What's our plan if it drives 70?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Dropbox** — Drew Houston's 3-minute screencast (2007) showed file sync working seamlessly across devices. Posted to Hacker News, drove 70,000 email signups in one day, validated demand, secured funding (pages 17, 81)
- **Buffer** — used a hybrid: explainer landing page + tweets describing the concept; validated before building (page 80)
- Many post-2007 startups — Dropbox-pattern videos became a standard pre-launch tactic
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Minimum Viable Product]] — _specializes_
- [[Landing Page MVP]] — _see_also_ (often paired)
- [[Fake Door Test]] — _see_also_
- [[Build-Measure-Learn Loop]] — _amplifies_
- [[Validated Learning]] — _see_also_

title: Exponential Business Model
definition: An exponential business model leverages technology, networks, and platforms to create and capture value at a rate that is disproportionately faster than the resources or effort invested. Pereira frames it as a deliberate shift away from the "pipe model" of linear, one-customer-at-a-time growth toward an architecture in which the business asset is increasingly information, software, network access, or an orchestrated ecosystem rather than physical inventory. The model is distinguished by three combined ingredients: (1) network effects that make the product more valuable as more people use it, (2) data and algorithms that compound advantage over time, and (3) platforms or ecosystems that bring multiple stakeholders together so the business does not need to own the underlying assets it monetizes. Airbnb (no rooms), Uber (no cars), Amazon (no proprietary inventory at the catalog layer), and Spotify (no recordings) are the canonical illustrations.

sections.Definition: An exponential business model leverages technology, networks, and platforms to create and capture value at a rate that is disproportionately faster than the resources or effort invested. Pereira frames it as a deliberate shift away from the "pipe model" of linear, one-customer-at-a-time growth toward an architecture in which the business asset is increasingly information, software, network access, or an orchestrated ecosystem rather than physical inventory. The model is distinguished by three combined ingredients: (1) network effects that make the product more valuable as more people use it, (2) data and algorithms that compound advantage over time, and (3) platforms or ecosystems that bring multiple stakeholders together so the business does not need to own the underlying assets it monetizes. Airbnb (no rooms), Uber (no cars), Amazon (no proprietary inventory at the catalog layer), and Spotify (no recordings) are the canonical illustrations.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Tech-leveraged value creation** — value is generated through software, automation, machine learning, and platforms rather than through physical scaling of plants, people, or inventory (pages 16-17)
- **Network effects baked in** — value to each user rises as more users join; defensibility compounds (pages 17, 42-43)
- **Asset-light architecture** — the firm orchestrates assets owned by others (rooms, cars, sellers, drivers, hosts) rather than buying them (pages 30, 71-72)
- **Data and algorithmic core** — operations, pricing, matching, and recommendations are algorithmically driven; data is the strategic moat (pages 17, 40)
- **Multi-sided platforms or ecosystems** — buyers/sellers, drivers/riders, hosts/travelers, developers/users (pages 46-47)
- **Disrupts incumbents while creating new markets** — cheaper, faster, more efficient than linear counterparts (page 18)
- **10x value differential** — winning ExOs offer not 10-20% improvement but a 10x leap on the most-valued dimension (page 32)
- **Lean methodology pairs naturally** — rapid iteration, fail-fast, continuous learning (pages 23-24, 39)
sections.When to use:
- The problem you are solving has a very large potential customer base (mass market)
- Value can be substantially mediated through software, data, or a network
- An incumbent industry is structurally tied to physical assets you could orchestrate instead of owning
- You can plausibly offer a 10x improvement on the buyer's most-valued attribute (price, speed, selection, convenience)
- You have the appetite and capital to invest ahead of revenue and tolerate first-mover risk
- The category benefits from positive feedback loops (more users → more value → more users)
sections.When NOT to use:
- The category is intrinsically physical, regulated, or trust-bound in ways that prevent platform mediation (e.g., highly specialized B2B services with long sales cycles, regulated medical procedures, bespoke craftsmanship)
- The customer base is small and unlikely to grow into a mass market — you cannot reach the volumes that exponential math requires
- You cannot identify any technology lever capable of producing a 10x differential
- You lack the runway, talent, or risk tolerance for the heavy upfront investment and the willingness to burn cash to acquire critical mass
- A linear, incremental "build a brand and a moat" approach would actually serve the founder's wealth goals better at lower risk (page 28)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Wishful-growth assumptions** — assuming continued exponential growth without modelling market shocks, supply-chain failures, or competitor responses (page 27)
- **Quality sacrificed for speed** — racing to ship multiple products and territories before any of them is loved by customers (page 27)
- **Massive overhead from "more is more"** — taking on inventory, marketing, and freight cost across many SKUs/territories without unit economics that scale (page 28)
- **Trying to retrofit an existing organization** — incumbent immune systems and existing assets distort the design; building from scratch usually beats retrofitting (page 80)
- **Confusing technology adoption with an ExO** — adding software to a linear model is not the same as designing the model around exponential leverage
- **Ignoring the 10x bar** — incremental improvements (10-20% better) do not produce exponential adoption (page 32)
- **Treating MTP as marketing copy** — without a real Massive Transformative Purpose the org cannot attract the talent or community required (page 81)
sections.Key metrics:
- **Market potential** — addressable users × willingness to pay
- **10x value differential** — your offer's improvement multiple vs the best incumbent option on the most-valued attribute (page 32)
- **CAC to LTV ratio** — viral/organic acquisition keeps CAC near zero (page 33)
- **Viral coefficient / referral rate** — how many new users each user brings in
- **Contribution margin near 100%** — software-style economics that scale without proportionate cost (page 34)
- **Revenue per employee** — exponential orgs run at 5-10x the revenue per head of linear peers
- **Active two-sided liquidity** — for platform variants: matched transactions per unit time
sections.Decision questions:
- Is the problem we solve big enough to justify exponential scale, or is it really a niche?
- Where in our value chain does technology create a 10x lever, not a 10% one?
- What asset would we orchestrate (instead of own) if we redesigned this category from scratch?
- Which network effect, if any, will compound for us — and what tips the flywheel into motion?
- Do our economics actually scale, or do costs grow in lockstep with revenue (a linear business in disguise)?
- If we cannot offer 10x improvement on at least one dimension, why will customers switch?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Airbnb** — short-term housing platform owning no hotels; connects travelers, hosts, experience providers (pages 30, 71-72, 80)
- **Uber** — two- and three-sided transportation/delivery platform; no fleet (pages 18, 72-73)
- **Amazon** — e-commerce platform, ecosystem, and infrastructure provider; AWS alone projected to reach $100B (pages 38-39, 63-64, 76)
- **Spotify** — streaming platform with freemium-to-subscription conversion ~45%; two-sided artist/listener market (pages 56, 77)
- **Netflix** — subscription streaming, $29.6B revenue in 2021 (pages 52, 75-76)
- **PayPal** — two-sided payments platform, >200M users (page 73)
- **Etsy** — marketplace, 88.3M active buyers / 5.3M active sellers in 2022 (page 74)
- **Squarespace** — presence + commerce SaaS with custom monetization tier (page 75)
- **Google** — algorithmic core (PageRank), >90% search share, ecosystem play (pages 40, 45, 64-65)
- **Waze** — community-contributed mapping; acquired by Google for $1.3B with 100 employees (page 38)

title: Fake Door Test
definition: A Fake Door test (also called "audience-building MVP" or "MVP before the MVP") is a low-fidelity validation tactic where you advertise a feature or product that doesn't exist yet, then measure how many people try to engage with it — by clicking, signing up, or attempting to purchase. The "door" looks real, but when opened it reveals a message that the functionality is "under construction" or asks the user to enter their email to be notified. Pereira positions it as a way to "gauge interest in a theoretical feature, without having to build that feature or product." The user's click is a real behavioral signal of latent demand.

sections.Definition: A Fake Door test (also called "audience-building MVP" or "MVP before the MVP") is a low-fidelity validation tactic where you advertise a feature or product that doesn't exist yet, then measure how many people try to engage with it — by clicking, signing up, or attempting to purchase. The "door" looks real, but when opened it reveals a message that the functionality is "under construction" or asks the user to enter their email to be notified. Pereira positions it as a way to "gauge interest in a theoretical feature, without having to build that feature or product." The user's click is a real behavioral signal of latent demand.
sections.Key attributes:
- **The offer doesn't exist** — but the user doesn't know that at click-time
- **Behavioral signal, not stated preference** — measures what people DO, not what they say
- **Email capture as the gentle redirect** — turns disappointed clickers into a waitlist
- **Cheap to deploy** — a CTA, a redirect page, and email capture
- **Can be embedded in an existing product** — test a feature within your live UI
- **Ethically delicate** — you're showing something that isn't real; framing matters
- **Distinct from Landing Page MVP** — Fake Door specifically tests a feature against an existing offer or product
sections.When to use:
- Inside an existing product, to test demand for a new feature before building it
- For pricing-tier validation (test if a Premium plan would convert before building it)
- When stakeholders disagree about whether to build feature X
- When engineering cost of the feature is high and demand is uncertain
- For prioritizing among multiple proposed features (run several Fake Doors, compare conversion)
sections.When NOT to use:
- For your overall company offer (use Landing Page MVP instead)
- In contexts where the user reaction to discovering the door is fake would damage trust badly (high-trust B2B, regulated)
- When you can't follow up with the people who clicked (the email list is the whole asset)
- When the feature is small enough to just build and A/B test
- For features that depend on viral/network effects (the test misrepresents the experience)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **No follow-up on the waitlist** — collected emails go cold, opportunity wasted
- **Negative user experience** — users feel tricked if the "under construction" framing is hostile
- **Confusing click with intent** — many users click out of curiosity, not real demand
- **Low traffic = no signal** — small samples produce noisy conversion data
- **Not pre-committing a conversion threshold** — moving goalposts after seeing data
- **Running too many Fake Doors at once** — feature-test fatigue erodes user trust
- **Misattributing the click** — was it the feature concept, the badge color, or the location?
sections.Key metrics:
- Click-through rate on the fake door
- Email capture rate among those who clicked
- Compared baseline conversion vs the surrounding product
- Cost per validated lead (if running via paid traffic)
- Source-level breakdown if you're testing multiple variants
sections.Decision questions:
- What conversion threshold, set in advance, would justify building this feature?
- Is the click-driving signal coming from real intent — or from misleading positioning?
- What's our promise to people who clicked, and can we honor it?
- Are we testing the feature, the description, or the call-to-action — and have we isolated which?
- Do we have a polite, trust-preserving redirect message ready?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Premium subscription test (Pereira illustration, page 32-33)** — landing page with full premium-plan description, CTA leads to "under construction" message with email capture; measures willingness to pay for the upgraded tier before building it
- **Buffer feature tests** — historically used Fake Doors inside the app to test which premium features customers would click on
- **Many B2C SaaS** — embedded "Coming Soon" CTAs for new features to gauge demand pre-build

- [[Landing Page MVP]] — _see_also_ (broader cousin, tests the whole offer)
- [[Build-Measure-Learn Loop]] — _amplifies_
- [[Validated Learning]] — _see_also_

title: Falsifiable Hypothesis Building
definition: A falsifiable hypothesis is a specific, testable claim about a business model assumption that can be definitively confirmed or refuted by evidence. Pereira's method: (1) Identify the assumption underlying a BMC block; (2) Define the customer segment with whom to test; (3) Determine a specific metric that would prove or disprove the hypothesis. Falsifiability — Popper's scientific criterion — is what separates a real test from confirmation-bias-prone "validation." A good hypothesis has a clear failure condition; if you can't articulate how you'd know you're wrong, you're not testing.

sections.Definition: A falsifiable hypothesis is a specific, testable claim about a business model assumption that can be definitively confirmed or refuted by evidence. Pereira's method: (1) Identify the assumption underlying a BMC block; (2) Define the customer segment with whom to test; (3) Determine a specific metric that would prove or disprove the hypothesis. Falsifiability — Popper's scientific criterion — is what separates a real test from confirmation-bias-prone "validation." A good hypothesis has a clear failure condition; if you can't articulate how you'd know you're wrong, you're not testing.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Specific, not vague** — "20% of doctors download the app" beats "doctors will like the app"
- **Falsifiable** — must be possible to disprove with data
- **Bounded to a segment** — applies to a defined customer group, not "the market"
- **Time-bounded** — typically expressed with a deadline ("within 30 days of launch")
- **Measurable** — has a concrete metric that defines success or failure
- **Pre-registered** — set the success threshold BEFORE the test, not after
sections.When to use:
- Step 3 of the Business Model Testing Cycle
- Before any A/B test, MVP, or experiment
- For investor pitches — convert claims into falsifiable predictions
- For team alignment — falsifiable hypotheses force shared mental models
- Post-launch — examine which hypotheses held and which didn't
sections.When NOT to use:
- For purely qualitative research (interviews, observation) where the goal is discovery
- When you can't actually measure the outcome
- In domains where ethical/operational constraints prevent the test
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Vague hypotheses that can't be falsified ("customers will like our product")
- Setting success thresholds AFTER seeing results (post-hoc rationalization)
- Confirmation bias: stopping the test as soon as data supports the hypothesis
- Testing too many things simultaneously (can't attribute results)
- Falsification: refusing to accept disconfirming evidence
sections.Key metrics:
- Hypothesis specificity score (would a stranger know exactly what to measure?)
- Success criterion clarity (pre-registered vs. post-hoc)
- Validation rate (% of hypotheses that held vs. were refuted)
- Time-to-test per hypothesis
sections.Decision questions:
- For each hypothesis: what specific data would convince me I'm wrong?
- Did we set the success threshold before starting the test, or after?
- Are we testing one variable at a time, or confounding multiple?
- When the test refutes our hypothesis, will we accept it or rationalize?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **App for doctors** — "20% of doctors in town will download the app within 60 days of launch" (page 12)
- **Subscription product** — "10% of free-tier users will upgrade within 30 days"
- **Pricing test** — "50% conversion rate at $9.99 vs. 35% at $19.99"
- **Feature adoption** — "30% of users will use the new feature within 14 days"
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Business Model Testing Cycle]] — _specializes_ (this is Step 3)
- [[Continuous Improvement]] — _amplifies_
- [[Customer Feedback Loop]] — _see_also_

title: Feature Matrix Analysis
definition: A feature matrix is a structured side-by-side comparison of products or services across a defined set of features, used to visualize differences between competitors and inform product, positioning, and pricing decisions. Each row is typically a feature; each column is a competitor (including your own offering). Pereira describes the matrix as essential for any competitor analysis, with each entry including the feature type, a concise description, and a benefit statement explaining why the feature matters to the customer. The matrix scales beyond raw features to include customer reviews, support options, and policy details — giving "a comprehensive view of the competitive landscape."

sections.Definition: A feature matrix is a structured side-by-side comparison of products or services across a defined set of features, used to visualize differences between competitors and inform product, positioning, and pricing decisions. Each row is typically a feature; each column is a competitor (including your own offering). Pereira describes the matrix as essential for any competitor analysis, with each entry including the feature type, a concise description, and a benefit statement explaining why the feature matters to the customer. The matrix scales beyond raw features to include customer reviews, support options, and policy details — giving "a comprehensive view of the competitive landscape."
sections.Key attributes:
- **Side-by-side format** — rows = features, columns = competitors (or vice versa)
- **Three required fields per feature** — feature type/name, description (with technical specifics decoded), benefit (why it matters to customers)
- **Extends beyond features** — Pereira's version also includes customer reviews, support options, payment methods, security/privacy policies
- **Customer-priority weighting** — features should be evaluated against what target customers actually value, not internal pride
- **Visual side-by-side** — the spatial layout itself surfaces gaps that prose would obscure
- **Drives invest/cut decisions** — used to "invest appropriately in those areas while avoiding costly overspending on unnecessary bells and whistles"
- **Living document** — refreshed as competitor offerings evolve
sections.When to use:
- Building product roadmaps where competitor parity vs. differentiation must be balanced
- Sales enablement — equipping the sales team with crisp comparison talking points
- Pricing decisions — feature comparison underpins value-based pricing arguments
- Market entry assessment — what's the table-stakes feature set?
- After a competitor launch — quickly assess whether you need to respond
- Customer research synthesis — map "must-have" features customers requested to existing offerings
sections.When NOT to use:
- When the value proposition isn't feature-driven (luxury, identity, ecosystem lock-in often outweigh feature parity)
- For commoditized categories where features are functionally identical and price/service is the real game
- When the matrix becomes a vanity exercise listing minor features customers don't care about
- As a substitute for qualitative customer research — features matter, but *which* features matter requires customer voice
- For services where the "feature" abstraction breaks down (experience, trust, expertise don't fit neatly)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Feature inflation** — adding rows for every micro-feature, drowning the meaningful ones
- **Internal-view bias** — listing features your team is proud of rather than features customers value
- **No benefit column** — listing features without translating them to customer outcomes
- **Missing the customer-priority weighting** — checkmark parity doesn't mean equivalent customer value
- **Outdated entries** — competitor offerings evolve; stale matrices misinform
- **Defensive feature-matching** — building features to match competitors rather than to win customers
- **Ignoring reviews and policies** — features are only half the story; trust signals, support, terms also drive choice
- **Confusing feature count with product superiority** — more checkboxes ≠ better product
sections.Key metrics:
- **Number of features compared** — calibrate to "meaningful to customers," not "exhaustive"
- **Feature parity score** — % of competitor features you match
- **Unique features count** — your features no competitor has (true differentiation)
- **Customer-priority alignment** — % of top-N customer-stated needs covered
- **Feature-to-outcome ratio** — features that map to measurable customer outcomes
- **Refresh interval** — quarterly for fast-moving categories, semi-annually otherwise
sections.Decision questions:
- Which features are table stakes (everyone has them, customers expect them)?
- Which features are true differentiators (we have, others don't)?
- Which competitor features should we copy, vs. consciously avoid, vs. counter with something different?
- Are we investing in features customers actually value, or features that look good in marketing?
- What's the cost of feature parity vs. the cost of differentiation in another dimension entirely?
- If a customer compared us side-by-side today, which row would close the deal?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Pereira's recommended feature-matrix dimensions** — usability/convenience, pricing, customization, security/privacy, support services, UX design, platforms/devices supported, speed/bandwidth, data storage availability (page 20)
- **Sony PS5 vs. Nintendo Switch implicit feature matrix** — Pereira contrasts PS5's superior graphics and processing vs. Switch's portability and versatility; this is feature-matrix logic narrated rather than gridded (page 17-18)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Competitive Analysis Process]] — _specializes_ (feature matrix is a tactical sub-tool within the broader process)
- [[Competitive Product Analysis]] — _see_also_ (the product-level method that often uses feature matrices)
- [[7Ps Marketing Mix]] — _see_also_ (feature matrix lives within the "Product" P of the broader 7Ps analysis)
- [[Perceptual Mapping]] — _see_also_ (feature data often feeds attribute selection for perceptual maps)
- [[SWOT Analysis]] — _see_also_ (feature gaps surface in the Weaknesses quadrant of competitor SWOTs)

title: First Principles Thinking
definition: First Principles Thinking is the practice of decomposing a hard problem down to its irreducible facts — what is provably true — and then reasoning back up from there, rather than reasoning by analogy or precedent. Pereira frames it as "one of the most recommended ways to find out how things work and open up creative possibilities," noting it is sometimes called "reasoning from first principles." The discipline strips away inherited assumptions, exposes the basic elements, and lets new solutions emerge from a clean foundation — Elon Musk's signature method for attacking SpaceX, Tesla, and PayPal.

sections.Definition: First Principles Thinking is the practice of decomposing a hard problem down to its irreducible facts — what is provably true — and then reasoning back up from there, rather than reasoning by analogy or precedent. Pereira frames it as "one of the most recommended ways to find out how things work and open up creative possibilities," noting it is sometimes called "reasoning from first principles." The discipline strips away inherited assumptions, exposes the basic elements, and lets new solutions emerge from a clean foundation — Elon Musk's signature method for attacking SpaceX, Tesla, and PayPal.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Decomposition** — break the problem into the most basic facts/elements that cannot be reduced further
- **Assumption audit** — explicitly write down every assumption, then challenge each one by considering its opposite
- **Reasoning forward, not by analogy** — build solutions from the base facts, not from what competitors or precedent suggest
- **Question-driven** — relies on potent, naive-sounding questions ("Why?", "Why is that true?", "What must be true for this to hold?")
- **Innovation-enabling** — by ignoring how things are "usually done," opens novel solution paths
- **Costly and slow** — requires significant cognitive effort; not suitable for routine or low-stakes decisions
- **Munger / Musk pedigree** — central to the Munger latticework and explicitly invoked by Musk: "Good ideas are crazy until they're not"
sections.When to use:
- High-stakes, novel, or strategic problems where the conventional answer is suspect
- Cost or design challenges where competitors have all converged on a similar (possibly inflated) solution
- Industry disruption / category-creation contexts (Tesla on battery cost, SpaceX on rocket reusability)
- When you sense an assumption is doing too much work in the current model
- Product development where existing approaches are heavy with legacy constraints
sections.When NOT to use:
- Routine, low-stakes decisions where analogical reasoning is faster and good enough
- Domains where you lack the expertise to identify what the "first principles" actually are
- Time-critical operational moments — first-principles reasoning is deliberately slow
- When the cost of being wrong is low and reversibility is high (just try the conventional thing)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Mistaking opinion or convention for a first principle (false floor)
- Decomposing too far — paralysis by infinite regress
- Re-inventing the wheel for problems where the conventional solution actually is right
- Hubris: assuming a fresh first-principles analysis beats deep domain expertise (often it doesn't)
- Confusing "ignoring how others do it" with "ignoring what is physically/economically true"
sections.Signals you're using it well:
- You can state the irreducible facts of the problem in plain language without invoking precedent
- Your solution differs structurally (not just incrementally) from the conventional approach
- You've explicitly challenged at least one assumption others treat as given
- The solution you propose would have been "crazy" to a peer six months ago
sections.Decision questions:
- What are the basic elements of this problem? What is provably true?
- Which of my assumptions am I treating as facts? What if the opposite were true?
- If I were starting from a blank sheet today, would I arrive at the current solution?
- Am I reasoning from first principles, or am I just copying what worked elsewhere?
- What would have to be true for a fundamentally different approach to win?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Elon Musk on rockets (SpaceX)** — instead of accepting market rocket prices, broke costs down to raw materials (aluminum, titanium, copper, carbon fiber) and found the materials cost was ~2% of the rocket price, opening reusability as the path to 10x cost reduction
- **Elon Musk on batteries (Tesla)** — same decomposition logic on lithium-ion battery cell cost
- **Aristotle** — credited as the original articulator of reasoning from the fewest possible postulates (Pereira, page 52)
- **Pereira's framing** — "Good ideas are crazy until they're not" (Musk, quoted page 32)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Second-Order Thinking]] — _see_also_ (first principles surfaces options; second-order evaluates their downstream effects)
- [[Inversion]] — _amplifies_ (inversion is a first-principles tool — reasoning from what would cause failure)
- [[Occam's Razor]] — _see_also_ (first principles often converge on the simplest explanation, but not always)
- [[Pre-Mortem Technique]] — _see_also_ (operational application of inverted first-principles reasoning)
- [[Map Is Not the Territory]] — _see_also_ (first principles guards against mistaking the inherited map for reality)
sections.Sources: - [[_Sources/Super_Guide_Mental_Models-omnxhn]] — pages 20 (six-core list), 31–32 (engaging in first-principles thinking: identify assumptions, separate into fundamentals, create new solutions)

title: Five Elements of Exponential Growth
definition: Pereira distills the diagnostic structure of any exponential business into five interlocking elements that must all be present for exponential math to actually function: market potential, customer value created, customer acquisition mechanics, customer lifetime value, and economies of scale. The framing functions both as a design tool (asking the team to design for each element) and as a diagnostic (asking why an existing ExO is not actually achieving exponential growth — almost always one or more of the five is weak). The conclusion chapter restates the same five as the "snowball recipe": an organic, low-cost customer acquisition model, supercharged by a partner ecosystem, high-margin products with lean overhead and economies of scale, and a problem solved for a large pool of potential customers (page 88).

sections.Definition: Pereira distills the diagnostic structure of any exponential business into five interlocking elements that must all be present for exponential math to actually function: market potential, customer value created, customer acquisition mechanics, customer lifetime value, and economies of scale. The framing functions both as a design tool (asking the team to design for each element) and as a diagnostic (asking why an existing ExO is not actually achieving exponential growth — almost always one or more of the five is weak). The conclusion chapter restates the same five as the "snowball recipe": an organic, low-cost customer acquisition model, supercharged by a partner ecosystem, high-margin products with lean overhead and economies of scale, and a problem solved for a large pool of potential customers (page 88).
sections.Key attributes:
- **Market Potential** — the addressable problem must be large enough that even modest per-customer revenue compounds to a big business; low price × huge users OR high price × narrower base (page 31)
- **Customer Value Created** — must be substantially better than competitors (the 10x factor); incremental improvement does not produce exponential adoption (page 32)
- **Customer Acquisition** — must be organic or viral; CAC must be a small fraction of revenue per customer (page 32-33)
- **Customer Lifetime Value** — sustained relationship plus per-customer revenue expansion over time; B2C via daily routine integration, B2B via process integration (page 33)
- **Economies of Scale** — designed in from day one; software-style ~100% marginal margins are the archetype (page 34)
- **All five together** — weakness in any single element undermines exponential math (page 88)
- **Sequential design order** — market potential is upstream; economies of scale are downstream
- **Recipe-grade combinability** — when all five align, the business compounds; when any is missing, it fizzles
sections.When to use:
- Designing a new venture and pressure-testing the model against each element
- Diagnosing why a venture that "should be" exponential is actually growing linearly
- Conducting investor diligence on a growth-stage company
- Stress-testing a corporate innovation portfolio — which projects satisfy all five elements vs. which are linear plays in exponential clothing
- Coaching a founder who can describe their product but not their growth math
sections.When NOT to use:
- As a checklist on a linear business — applying it produces a forced score and discourages a legitimate linear strategy
- Without honest customer data — the elements depend on real market signal, not internal guesses
- In very early-stage ventures before product exists — there is nothing to measure yet; the framework is for design and diagnosis, not for pre-product validation
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Overweighting one element** — many founders obsess over product (Customer Value) and neglect acquisition mechanics
- **Confusing market size with market potential** — TAM slides without willingness-to-pay analysis
- **Paid acquisition disguised as organic** — high CAC dressed up as "growth marketing" undermines the Acquisition element
- **CLV without retention investment** — assuming long customer lives without operating for them
- **Hidden cost scaling** — the model looks like SaaS but COGS scale with revenue (true linear hiding inside)
- **All five "OK"** — being mediocre on all five rarely compounds; the strongest ExOs are exceptional on at least three
sections.Key metrics:
- **Market potential** — addressable users × willingness to pay (with realistic price-elasticity assumption)
- **Value multiple vs incumbent** — the 10x factor, customer-verified (page 32)
- **CAC** and **CAC payback period** — the organic-acquisition test (page 33)
- **Viral coefficient / referral rate** — proxy for organic spread
- **CLV / LTV** — customer-lifetime gross profit (page 33)
- **CLV:CAC ratio** — target ≥ 3:1 for healthy unit economics
- **Contribution margin** — proxy for economies of scale; software-style targets near 100% (page 34)
- **Revenue per employee** — proxy for whether the model actually scales without headcount
sections.Decision questions:
- Is our addressable market large enough that exponential math even has room to run?
- Where is our 10x — and is it 10x as graded by us or by the customer?
- What is our CAC, and what % of it is paid vs organic? Is the trend right?
- How do we deepen the customer relationship over time, and what specifically drives expansion?
- Do our unit economics actually scale, or do costs grow with revenue?
- If we removed all paid acquisition tomorrow, what would happen to growth?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Software businesses generally** — ~100% marginal margins as the archetype of designed-in economies of scale (page 34)
- **Subscription-based ExOs** — CLV-led growth pattern; Spotify, Netflix, Amazon Prime (page 33)
- **Viral acquisition ExOs** — PayPal's referral pay-per-signup; Dropbox storage incentives
- **Ambassador-supported acquisition** — Pereira cites the combination of viral + ambassador as exemplary (page 33)
- **Amazon's economies of scale flywheel** — variety attracts customers, customers attract sellers, scale lowers cost per unit (pages 47, 87)
- **Airbnb's mass-market potential** — global travel demand × room owners with idle capacity (page 30)

title: Five Stages of Subscription Maturity
definition: A capability-maturity framework for organizations adopting or operating subscription business models. Pereira's five stages: (1) **Ad Hoc** — subscription as side experiment; manual billing; few customers; no clear strategy; (2) **Opportunistic** — recognizing subscription's value; pilots in specific segments; basic recurring billing; (3) **Repeatable** — subscription is a real revenue line; consistent pricing, billing, retention practices; (4) **Managed** — subscription is core to strategy; sophisticated analytics; segmentation; cross-sell/upsell motions; (5) **Optimized** — subscription is the operating model; data-driven pricing, predictive churn, AI-augmented customer success.

sections.Definition: A capability-maturity framework for organizations adopting or operating subscription business models. Pereira's five stages: (1) **Ad Hoc** — subscription as side experiment; manual billing; few customers; no clear strategy; (2) **Opportunistic** — recognizing subscription's value; pilots in specific segments; basic recurring billing; (3) **Repeatable** — subscription is a real revenue line; consistent pricing, billing, retention practices; (4) **Managed** — subscription is core to strategy; sophisticated analytics; segmentation; cross-sell/upsell motions; (5) **Optimized** — subscription is the operating model; data-driven pricing, predictive churn, AI-augmented customer success.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Five sequential stages** — Ad Hoc → Opportunistic → Repeatable → Managed → Optimized
- **Capability accumulates** — each stage adds operational sophistication
- **Different metrics matter per stage** — early stages count subscribers; mature stages optimize cohort economics
- **Tooling differs** — Ad Hoc uses spreadsheets; Optimized uses Zuora/Recurly/Stripe Billing with custom analytics
- **Cultural shift required** — moving from product-sale culture to ongoing-service culture
- **Reverse possible** — orgs can regress when execution slips
sections.The five stages:
1. **Ad Hoc**: subscription as side experiment; manual processes; no formal pricing strategy; revenue is small fraction of business
2. **Opportunistic**: leadership sees subscription's value; pilots in specific segments; basic recurring billing infrastructure
3. **Repeatable**: subscription is a real revenue line; consistent pricing; standard billing system; retention practices documented
4. **Managed**: subscription is core to strategy; analytics on MRR/ARR/churn; segmented offerings; upsell/cross-sell motions; customer success function
5. **Optimized**: subscription is the operating model; predictive churn modeling; data-driven pricing; AI-augmented success motions; world-class NRR
sections.When to use:
- Strategic planning to identify current maturity and gaps
- Investment prioritization (which capabilities to build next)
- Comparing your subscription maturity to competitors
- Pre-IPO / pre-acquisition diligence on subscription operations
sections.When NOT to use:
- For non-subscription businesses (no fit)
- As a rigid roadmap (companies don't always progress linearly)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Pretending to be at Stage 4-5 while operating at 2-3
- Skipping stages (Ad Hoc → Managed without Repeatable infrastructure)
- Investing in optimization tooling before achieving repeatability
- Letting maturity slip (acquired teams often regress)
- Misjudging which stage you're at (most orgs overestimate)
sections.Key metrics:
- Revenue predictability (forecast accuracy)
- Billing infrastructure score (manual vs. automated)
- Churn management sophistication (reactive vs. predictive)
- Customer Success function maturity (none → reactive → proactive → predictive)
sections.Decision questions:
- Honestly assessed, what stage are we at?
- What would moving up one stage require (people, tools, process)?
- Where are we operating below our claimed stage?
- Which competitor is operating one stage above us, and what does that look like?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Stage 5 Optimized**: Salesforce, Adobe, Netflix — fully data-driven subscription operations
- **Stage 4 Managed**: most mature SaaS companies; have analytics + customer success
- **Stage 3 Repeatable**: subscription lines that work but lack sophisticated optimization
- **Stage 2 Opportunistic**: traditional companies (manufacturers, retailers) piloting subscription
- **Stage 1 Ad Hoc**: most small businesses' first subscription attempts

title: Five Steps to Design an Exponential Business Model
definition: This is Pereira's prescribed five-step process for designing a new exponential business from a blank page. It is written for two audiences simultaneously: the founder building a new ExO from scratch, and the intrapreneur attempting to incubate an exponential venture on the edge of an established organization. The author is explicit that retrofitting an existing business model is a trap — incumbent assets and organizational immune systems will distort the design. The process therefore begins with deliberate detachment from existing assets and proceeds through MTP definition, problem/solution fit, ExO Canvas design, and BMC alignment. The worked example used throughout the chapter is "What if Hilton tried to invent Airbnb?" — illustrating both the power of the process and the difficulty of doing it inside a legacy organization.

sections.Definition: This is Pereira's prescribed five-step process for designing a new exponential business from a blank page. It is written for two audiences simultaneously: the founder building a new ExO from scratch, and the intrapreneur attempting to incubate an exponential venture on the edge of an established organization. The author is explicit that retrofitting an existing business model is a trap — incumbent assets and organizational immune systems will distort the design. The process therefore begins with deliberate detachment from existing assets and proceeds through MTP definition, problem/solution fit, ExO Canvas design, and BMC alignment. The worked example used throughout the chapter is "What if Hilton tried to invent Airbnb?" — illustrating both the power of the process and the difficulty of doing it inside a legacy organization.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Five sequential steps with iteration loops** — Start Fresh → Define MTP → Find Global Problem + Scalable Solution → Define ExO Canvas → Define BMC based on ExO Canvas (pages 79-83)
- **Detachment as step zero** — existing assets and culture must be set aside (page 80)
- **MTP comes before product** — the purpose is the design constraint, not the wrapper (page 81)
- **Global scope, technological solution** — solutions must be designed for worldwide reach using compound-tech leverage (page 82)
- **Two canvases in dialog** — the BMC and ExO Canvas iterate against each other (pages 83-85)
- **Edge-of-org placement for intrapreneurs** — new exponential models must be incubated outside the parent's operational and political reach (page 80)
- **Four-of-five attribute rule** — using at least four ExO attributes substantially raises odds of exponential growth (page 83)
- **Backtrack expected** — step 3 may fail; the author explicitly notes you should return and reformulate (page 82)
sections.When to use:
- A founder is starting a new venture and wants to design for exponential growth from day one
- A corporate innovation team is launching an "edge" venture deliberately outside the parent BU
- An existing business is being reimagined from scratch (clean-sheet exercise) for strategic planning
- A team needs structured guidance to move from "we want to build something big" to a concrete design
sections.When NOT to use:
- The venture is intrinsically linear (artisanal, regulated specialty, bespoke services) — the process will produce a bad design
- The team has no appetite to detach from existing assets and politics — go through the motions and you will produce theater
- The category genuinely lacks 10x technology leverage — better to acknowledge this and design a strong linear business
- You are mid-execution on a working model — the process is for design, not for in-flight pivots without strong cause
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Skipping Step 1 (Start Fresh)** — most failures originate here; teams quietly drag existing assumptions into the design
- **MTP as marketing copy** — defining the MTP as a tagline rather than a purpose powerful enough to attract talent and community (page 81)
- **Solving local problems** — the process is explicit that global problems are the substrate for exponential growth (page 82)
- **Treating Step 4 as a checklist** — ticking off all five ExO attributes without honest application
- **Skipping the BMC<->ExO oscillation** — one pass through each is not sufficient; iteration is the point (page 85)
- **Building inside the parent org without protection** — intrapreneurs who don't insulate the project from the corporate immune system get killed (page 80)
sections.Key metrics:
- **MTP quality** — does it actually scare you (Richard Branson test, cited on page 81)?
- **Global problem-solution fit** — independent validation that the problem is global, not regional
- **SCALE attributes engaged** — count and depth of ExO Canvas blocks populated
- **BMC-ExO alignment score** — do the two canvases tell the same story?
- **Iteration cycles completed** — proxy for design rigor
sections.Decision questions:
- Have we genuinely detached from existing assets, or are we still designing for the constraints we already know?
- Is our MTP big enough to attract the talent and community we will need?
- Is the problem we're solving global, and is the solution technologically scalable?
- For each ExO attribute, do we have a real plan or a placeholder?
- Have we run the BMC and ExO Canvas in dialog, or only side by side?
- If we're inside a parent org, have we secured the political insulation to actually execute?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Hilton-becomes-Airbnb worked example** — the chapter's running illustration: MTP ("everyone belongs everywhere"), problem (cold hotels, underused homes), solution (platform matching), ExO Canvas application (pages 80-83)
- **Airbnb's actual ExO Canvas** — referenced explicitly as the design exemplar (page 83)
- **General Electric's pivot** — applied lean methodology across core operations to enable exponential development (page 39)
- **OpenExO's process** — the source the author cites (blog.openexo.com) for the five-step methodology
sections.Related concepts:
- [[ExO Canvas]] — _operationalizes_ (the design tool used in step 4)
- [[Business Model Canvas]] — _see_also_ (the partner canvas used in step 5)
- [[Massive Transformative Purpose]] — _anchored_by_ (the output of step 2)
- [[Nine Design Principles for Exponential Transformation]] — _see_also_ (BMC-block-level design heuristics)
- [[Exponential Business Model]] — _see_also_ (the output type the process produces)
- [[Open Innovation]] — _see_also_ (the partnership pattern the process encourages)

title: Five-Step Value Creation Plan
definition: Pereira's Five-Step Value Creation Plan is a meta-framework that sequences the major moves a firm must make to deliberately create value: (1) Define Value Proposition, (2) Identify Target Customers, (3) Conduct Value Chain Analysis, (4) Implement Innovation Strategies, (5) Create Positive Social/Environmental Impact. Unlike piecemeal application of these concepts, the plan integrates them into a coherent sequence — each step depending on the prior. The source uses Tesla, Nike, Amazon, Apple, and Patagonia as anchor examples across the steps.

sections.Definition: Pereira's Five-Step Value Creation Plan is a meta-framework that sequences the major moves a firm must make to deliberately create value: (1) Define Value Proposition, (2) Identify Target Customers, (3) Conduct Value Chain Analysis, (4) Implement Innovation Strategies, (5) Create Positive Social/Environmental Impact. Unlike piecemeal application of these concepts, the plan integrates them into a coherent sequence — each step depending on the prior. The source uses Tesla, Nike, Amazon, Apple, and Patagonia as anchor examples across the steps.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Sequential dependency** — steps build on each other; skipping creates fragility
- **Five distinct moves** — each step is a known concept (VP, personas, VCA, innovation, sustainability) integrated into a flow
- **Integrative** — combines customer-facing, operational, and external-impact dimensions
- **Strategic plan output** — the deliverable is a coordinated strategic plan, not a list of unrelated initiatives
- **Tested by example** — each step has a canonical company demonstration in the source
- **Repeatable** — runs through the plan periodically as conditions shift
sections.When to use:
- Annual strategic planning when leadership wants a coordinated frame
- After a significant disruption or market shift requires re-baselining strategy
- When building a new business unit or entering a new market
- During a turnaround where the current strategy isn't coherent
- As a checklist to audit an existing strategy for missing dimensions
sections.When NOT to use:
- As a one-time exercise — the plan must be re-run as conditions evolve
- When the firm needs a deep dive into a single dimension (use the specific framework instead)
- When time pressure demands a faster framework — this is comprehensive but slow
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Treating the steps as a checklist to fill in rather than a strategy to act on
- Skipping Step 2 (target customers) and writing a generic VP — most common failure
- Doing Steps 1-4 financially and treating Step 5 (impact) as PR garnish
- Letting different teams own different steps without integration
- Not revisiting earlier steps when later analysis reveals contradictions
sections.Key metrics:
- Plan completion: each step has explicit, documented output
- Inter-step alignment score (does Step 3 actually serve Step 1?)
- Leadership-team alignment post-plan (shared mental model of strategy)
- Initiatives launched from the plan with assigned owners and metrics
sections.Decision questions:
- Have we actually defined our VP, or do we just have a tagline?
- Are our target customers the same as our actual customers? — if not, why?
- What did our value chain analysis change in our operations vs. before it?
- What innovation are we shipping this quarter that ties to the plan?
- What positive social/environmental impact do we measurably create, and is it credible?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Step 1 — Tesla** — value proposition defined as innovative, high-quality electric vehicles that are environmentally friendly and technologically advanced (page 32)
- **Step 2 — Nike** — identified target customers as athletes and fitness enthusiasts; designed innovative footwear and apparel for their specific needs (page 33)
- **Step 3 — Amazon** — used value chain analysis to engineer a highly efficient supply chain and seamless customer experience, becoming the e-commerce leader (page 33)
- **Step 4 — Apple** — implemented innovation strategy via iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, disrupting markets and creating growth (page 34)
- **Step 5 — Patagonia** — created positive societal and environmental impact through sustainable practices and environmental protection initiatives (page 34)

title: Fixed-Bid Project Pricing
definition: Fixed-bid (or fixed-fee) project pricing quotes the client one total price for a defined scope of work, regardless of how many hours the firm spends delivering it. Pereira derives the worked example for consulting on pages 23-25: estimate the underlying cost (direct labor + allocated overhead), add the target margin (e.g. 25%), and that becomes the lump-sum fee — but the client never sees the cost build-up, only the final number. The model **shifts delivery risk from the client to the provider**: overruns eat margin, efficiency gains create margin. McKinsey's "project-based" engagement model (page 121) is the institutional version. Fixed-bid only works when scope can be defined tightly enough that the provider can estimate effort with confidence and the client can verify completion.

sections.Definition: Fixed-bid (or fixed-fee) project pricing quotes the client one total price for a defined scope of work, regardless of how many hours the firm spends delivering it. Pereira derives the worked example for consulting on pages 23-25: estimate the underlying cost (direct labor + allocated overhead), add the target margin (e.g. 25%), and that becomes the lump-sum fee — but the client never sees the cost build-up, only the final number. The model **shifts delivery risk from the client to the provider**: overruns eat margin, efficiency gains create margin. McKinsey's "project-based" engagement model (page 121) is the institutional version. Fixed-bid only works when scope can be defined tightly enough that the provider can estimate effort with confidence and the client can verify completion.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Scope-locked price** — fee is set before delivery and does not adjust for actual hours spent
- **Delivery risk on the provider** — overruns reduce margin; efficiency creates margin
- **Margin upside from productivity** — templates, AI, reusable IP all flow directly to bottom line
- **Forces tight scoping** — a clear statement of work is a prerequisite, not an afterthought
- **Client gets budget certainty** — one number for finance, one PO, no surprises
- **Vulnerable to scope creep** — every "small addition" is unbilled unless change-orders are enforced
- **Requires accurate estimation discipline** — bad estimates compound into chronic margin loss
sections.When to use:
- Scope is well-understood (the firm has done similar engagements before)
- Client values budget certainty and a clean PO/contract over flexibility
- The firm has productivity advantages (templates, frameworks, AI) it wants to monetize
- Deliverable is concrete and verifiable (a strategy doc, a built system, a launched campaign)
- Sales cycle benefits from a clean, comparable number against competitors
sections.When NOT to use:
- Scope is genuinely fluid or discovery-driven (use T&M or phased fixed-bids)
- The firm has no historical data to estimate from (you're guessing at margin)
- Client is a serial scope-creeper without a culture of formal change requests
- Project depends heavily on client inputs or decisions outside the firm's control
- The work would be better priced on outcomes (use value-based pricing instead)
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Underestimating effort** — optimism bias eats the margin; track estimate vs actual ruthlessly
- **Accepting scope creep silently** — the polite "yes" to small additions kills profitability
- **Failing the change-order discipline** — needs a documented, low-friction way to re-price additions
- **Pricing from cost when value justifies more** — fixed-bid still ignores willingness-to-pay unless you anchor on value first
- **Loss-leader bidding** — winning the logo at zero margin rarely recovers in expansion
- **No phasing on uncertain work** — better to fix-bid Phase 1 (diagnostic) and rebid Phase 2 (delivery) than guess at both
- **Ignoring client-side delays** — clauses around client responsiveness are essential
sections.Key metrics:
- **Margin per project** — realized $ minus delivered cost, the only number that matters
- **Estimate accuracy** — actual hours ÷ estimated hours (drift toward 1.0 is the goal)
- **Scope creep rate** — % of projects that hit unplanned scope additions
- **Change-order capture rate** — % of additions that get re-priced vs absorbed
- **Win rate on fixed-bid proposals** — vs. T&M, vs. competitors
- **Realized vs quoted margin** — the gap reveals estimation and scoping discipline
sections.Decision questions:
- Do we have enough historical analogs to estimate this scope with confidence?
- Is our productivity edge real enough that fixed-bid is a strategic moat, not a gamble?
- What's our change-order discipline like — will we actually invoice the additions?
- Should we phase this (small fixed-bid diagnostic, then fixed-bid delivery)?
- Are we anchoring price on cost-plus, or on the value to the client?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Pereira consulting firm example** — $50,000 cost + 25% margin = $62,500 fixed fee on a strategic advisory project (pages 23-25)
- **McKinsey project-based engagement** — one of three engagement models McKinsey offers alongside retainer and performance-based (page 121)
- **IDEO design sprints** — scoped fixed-fee design engagements
- **Boutique strategy firms** — sell named workshops or playbooks at a list price (productized fixed-bid)
- **Agency website builds** — fixed quote for a defined site spec
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Hourly and Time-and-Materials Pricing]] — _contrasts_with_ (risk flips from provider to client)
- [[Value-Based Services Pricing]] — _see_also_ (a more sophisticated way to anchor the fixed number)
- [[Milestone-Based Payment]] — _see_also_ (how to phase cash collection within a fixed-bid)
- [[Productized Services]] — _see_also_ (fixed-bid taken to its logical extreme)
- [[Three Pricing Strategy Approaches]] — _specializes_

title: Flywheel Business Model
definition: The flywheel business model is a strategic frame that replaces the linear marketing funnel (attract → convert → close) with a self-reinforcing cycle centered on the existing customer. Pereira describes a three-stage cycle in which each satisfied customer becomes a source of new customers — via referrals, organic content, or the sheer attractive force of a growing network. The defining property is compounding: spin energy that goes into the wheel is conserved and accumulates, so each turn of the cycle requires less push than the last. The model is closely related to network effects but operates at the level of business choreography, not just product mechanics: it asks the organization to organize around customer delight as the primary growth lever rather than acquisition spend.

sections.Definition: The flywheel business model is a strategic frame that replaces the linear marketing funnel (attract → convert → close) with a self-reinforcing cycle centered on the existing customer. Pereira describes a three-stage cycle in which each satisfied customer becomes a source of new customers — via referrals, organic content, or the sheer attractive force of a growing network. The defining property is compounding: spin energy that goes into the wheel is conserved and accumulates, so each turn of the cycle requires less push than the last. The model is closely related to network effects but operates at the level of business choreography, not just product mechanics: it asks the organization to organize around customer delight as the primary growth lever rather than acquisition spend.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Customer at the center of the cycle** — every stage is designed around the existing customer's experience (page 86)
- **Three-stage compounding loop** — satisfy → retain → attract via referrals (page 86)
- **Self-reinforcing energy** — past spin reduces the energy required for future spin (page 86)
- **Pre-internet roots** — telephone networks and Yellow Pages were proto-flywheels (page 86)
- **Internet-age archetypes** — Amazon (more shoppers → more vendors → more variety → more shoppers); Facebook (friends invite friends); Uber (drivers attract riders attract drivers); Venmo (friends already have it) (page 87)
- **Community is the seed** — Pereira's prescription for implementation: start by creating the community (page 87)
- **Multiple loops, not one** — mature businesses operate several reinforcing flywheels in parallel
- **Asymmetric to launch** — flywheels are slow to start and dangerous to stop once moving
sections.When to use:
- Customer retention and referrals can plausibly drive a meaningful share of new acquisition
- Your category benefits from word-of-mouth, social proof, or network density
- You have or can build a community around the product
- You are willing to invest in customer satisfaction as a strategic lever, not a customer-service cost line
- The product has high enough quality that customers actually want to refer it
sections.When NOT to use:
- The product is one-shot or low-frequency (customers won't be around to refer)
- Customers buy in stealth or do not talk about the category socially
- The market is so small that referral compounding cannot scale
- Quality is too low to survive word-of-mouth (negative loops are just as compounding)
- Acquisition economics work fine without flywheel mechanics — adding flywheel ceremony just slows decisions
sections.Common pitfalls:
- **Calling a funnel a flywheel** — renaming a linear acquisition machine "a flywheel" without changing the architecture
- **No mechanism for referral** — relying on customers being "delighted" without giving them tools or incentives to share
- **Underinvesting in customer success** — flywheels fail at the satisfaction stage long before the referral stage
- **Stopping spin during downturns** — cutting the satisfaction investments that produce future free acquisition is the classic mistake
- **Single-flywheel fragility** — relying on one loop that a competitor can break (e.g., supply concentration)
- **Confusing flywheel with viral coefficient** — virality is one mechanism; the flywheel encompasses all compounding loops
sections.Key metrics:
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — leading indicator of referral propensity
- **Referral rate** — % of new customers attributable to existing customer behavior
- **Repeat purchase rate** — proxy for satisfaction stage
- **Cycle velocity** — time from first purchase to first referral
- **Compounding-advantage index** — declining CAC over time as flywheel matures
- **Community size and engagement** — for community-seeded flywheels
sections.Decision questions:
- What are our top three flywheels — and which are actually compounding vs. stalled?
- Where in the cycle do we lose energy (satisfaction gap? referral mechanism missing? onboarding friction for new customers from referrals?)
- What is the smallest investment that would speed the cycle by one full turn?
- If we cut customer-success spend by 30%, which flywheel breaks first — and how long until we feel it?
- Do we have a community, and if so, is it producing the referral lift we assume?
- Which competitor flywheels are accelerating against us, and where can we intervene?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Telephone** — utility rose with every new subscriber on the network (page 86)
- **Yellow Pages** — more businesses listed → more readers → more businesses listed → more ad revenue (page 86)
- **Amazon** — more shoppers → more vendors → more variety → competitive pricing → more shoppers (pages 47, 87)
- **Facebook** — joining members invite friends, growing the graph (page 87)
- **Uber** — more drivers → more riders → more drivers (page 87)
- **Venmo** — your friends already have it, so you get it, so your friends' friends get it (page 87)
- **Google** — more searches → more data → better results → more searches (page 45)

- [[Pareto Principle]] — _see_also_ (small share of customers drives disproportionate referrals)

title: Flywheel Effect
definition: The Flywheel Effect is a strategic framework describing how a company achieves sustainable, compounding growth through a continuous cycle of positive feedback rather than a one-shot push. Popularized by Jim Collins in *Good to Great*, the metaphor casts the business as a heavy flywheel: each small, consistent action adds momentum, and once the wheel is spinning, prior turns make every subsequent turn easier. Pereira frames it as a deliberately-engineered virtuous cycle where customer experience, retention, and advocacy feed back into acquisition — replacing the linear "fill the top, lose at the bottom" funnel with a self-reinforcing system that scales nonlinearly over time.

sections.Definition: The Flywheel Effect is a strategic framework describing how a company achieves sustainable, compounding growth through a continuous cycle of positive feedback rather than a one-shot push. Popularized by Jim Collins in *Good to Great*, the metaphor casts the business as a heavy flywheel: each small, consistent action adds momentum, and once the wheel is spinning, prior turns make every subsequent turn easier. Pereira frames it as a deliberately-engineered virtuous cycle where customer experience, retention, and advocacy feed back into acquisition — replacing the linear "fill the top, lose at the bottom" funnel with a self-reinforcing system that scales nonlinearly over time.
sections.Key attributes:
- **Compounding momentum** — each turn makes the next turn easier; growth is exponential rather than linear
- **Circular, not linear** — outputs (delighted customers) become inputs (referrals, repeat business)
- **Three operating phases** — *Build* momentum, *Maintain* momentum, *Accelerate* momentum
- **Customer experience at the core** — quality of CX is the load-bearing component
- **Continuous improvement bias** — small consistent wins beat episodic heroics
- **Cross-functional alignment** — sales, marketing, and service must push the same wheel
- **Long horizon** — the framework rewards patience; short-term thinking stalls it
- **Reversible** — neglected flywheels become Doom Loops (decay is symmetric to growth)
sections.When to use:
- Building a long-term growth engine where compounding > episodic campaigns
- Categories where customer experience is the primary driver of acquisition (referral-heavy)
- Two-sided marketplaces, network-effect platforms, and SaaS where retention amplifies new logos
- Organizations trying to align sales, marketing, and customer service around a single cycle
- Migrating from a transactional funnel mindset to a relationship/lifecycle mindset
- Startups choosing where to invest scarce resources for the highest leverage loop
sections.When NOT to use:
- Pure one-shot transactions with no realistic repeat or referral motion
- Crisis turnarounds that need immediate cash, not 18-month momentum
- Markets where the value proposition is undifferentiated (no "edge" to spin around)
- Organizations unwilling to commit to long-term consistency
- Categories where customer advocacy is structurally impossible (regulated, anonymous, one-time)
- When leadership cannot maintain discipline — flywheels fail under strategy-of-the-month
sections.Common pitfalls:
- Treating the flywheel as a slogan rather than a designed system of inputs and outputs
- Interrupting the wheel by chasing every new tactic ("never interrupt the flywheel")
- Falling into the Enterprise Trap — pursuing scale at the cost of the core edge
- Underinvesting in customer success, then watching retention compound the wrong way
- Failing to define the virtuous cycle explicitly (no shared mental model = no alignment)
- Pushing in multiple directions at once instead of pushing hard in one
- Mistaking early flat results for failure and abandoning before momentum builds
sections.Key metrics:
- **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** and **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — quality of CX
- **Customer Retention Rate** / **Churn Rate** — does the wheel hold the mass it gains?
- **Repeat purchase rate / cohort retention curves** — proof of compounding
- **First Response Time** and **Average Resolution Time** — friction inside the wheel
- **Conversion Rate** at each phase (Attract → Engage → Delight)
- **Word-of-mouth referral rate** — the flywheel's signature output
- **Loop velocity** — how fast a new customer becomes an advocate
sections.Decision questions:
- What is our single virtuous cycle, stated in one sentence with inputs and outputs?
- Which one input, if doubled, would accelerate every other component?
- Where are the friction points slowing the wheel right now?
- Are sales, marketing, and customer service measured against the same outcome?
- Are we in Phase 1 (build), Phase 2 (maintain), or Phase 3 (accelerate)?
- What would interrupt our flywheel — and have we ruled those moves out?
sections.Canonical examples:
- **Amazon** — lower prices → larger selection → better CX → more traffic → more sellers → lower prices (page 25)
- **HubSpot** — Attract → Engage → Delight inbound marketing flywheel (page 26)
- **Slack** — ease of use → integrations → network effect (page 27)
- **Airbnb** — hosts ↔ guests ↔ experiences community flywheel (page 28)
- **Uber** — drivers ↔ riders ↔ reduced wait times two-sided marketplace flywheel (page 28)
- **Netflix** — original content + personalization flywheel (page 29)
- **Vanguard** — low-cost products + high-value service flywheel (page 30)
sections.Related concepts:
- [[Virtuous Cycle]] — _specializes_ (the engine inside every flywheel)
- [[Network Effects]] — _amplifies_ (network-effect platforms have natural flywheels)
- [[Marketing Funnel]] — _contrasts_ (linear funnel vs circular flywheel)
- [[Doom Loop]] — _see_also_ (the flywheel running in reverse)
- [[Hedgehog Concept]] — _see_also_ (Collins's "what you can be best at" feeds flywheel focus)
- [[Customer Experience]] — _see_also_ (the load-bearing component)
- [[Continuous Improvement]] — _see_also_ (the operating discipline)
